| Aspect | Indirect Adaptive PP | Direct Adaptive PP |
|---|---|---|
| Identification | Plant model $(A, B)$ | Controller parameters $(R, S)$ |
| Design step | Solves Diophantine each sample | Direct parameter update law |
| Robustness | **High** (plant model checkable) | Lower (no plant estimate) |
| Computation | Higher | Lower |

**Indirect — Advantages (3.5 marks):**
1. **Interpretability:** The estimated $A, B$ are physically meaningful; the engineer can verify plant dynamics.
2. **Robustness to plant variations:** Changes in plant are detected and the controller is redesigned.

**Direct — Advantages (3.5 marks):**
1. **Computational simplicity:** No Diophantine solve; only a recursive update.
2. **Suitable for non-minimum-phase plants:** Bypasses the need to factor $B$ and check zeros.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $q^{-1}$ delay** in the plant model: Many students write $A y = B u$ without the one-step delay. This shifts the pole-placement algebra and the examiner will deduct **1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Wrong degree assignment for $R$:** Setting $\deg(R) = n_a$ instead of $n_a - 1$ leads to an *under-determined* system with non-unique solutions and the examiner's key marks your answer as incomplete. Deduct **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Omitting the tracking polynomial $T$:** Step tracking is part of the design. If $T$ is missing, expect a **1.5-mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Confusing RLS forgetting factor $\lambda$ with adaptive gain:** $\lambda$ is a *memory discount factor*, not a learning rate. Mixing up these notations costs **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Not verifying the result:** The closing line of every KTU solution should restate the closed-loop pole(s) and confirm they match the desired locations. Skipping verification typically costs **1 mark**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Box Before the Exam)**

- [ ] **Diophantine Equation** is the heart of pole placement: $AR + BS = A_c$.
- [ ] **Minimum-degree solution** requires $\deg(R) = n_a - 1$ and $\deg(S) = n_b$.
- [ ] **Closed-loop polynomial** has degree $\deg(A_c) = 2n_a - 1$ in the minimum-degree case.
- [ ] **RST structure** uses three polynomials: $R$ in feedback, $S$ in feedback, $T$ in feedforward.
- [ ] **Tracking polynomial $T(1) = A_c(1)/B(1)$** ensures zero steady-state error to step input.
- [ ] **Certainty Equivalence Principle:** design with $\hat{\theta}(t)$ as if it were true.
- [ ] **Self-Tuning Regulator** = Recursive Estimator + Diophantine Solver + RST Controller.
- [ ] **RLS update** uses $\lambda \in [0.95, 0.99]$ (forgetting factor) to track time-varying plants.
- [ ] **s-to-z mapping:** $z = e^{s T_s}$; for $\zeta = 0.707$ and $\omega_n$, place poles inside the unit circle.
- [ ] **Indirect adaptive PP** estimates plant first; **Direct adaptive PP** estimates controller directly.
- [ ] **Plant must be stabilizable:** $B(q^{-1})$ should have no unstable zeros if perfect tracking is required.
- [ ] **Equation count = Unknown count:** This is the *uniqueness* condition for minimum-degree design.
- [ ] **Always verify** the closed-loop transfer function $B T / A_c$ after solving the Diophantine equation.
- [ ] **Industrial name:** RST is the *de facto* standard in digital industrial controllers (e.g., Schneider, ABB).
- [ ] **Disturbance rejection** is governed by $R/S$ alone; **Tracking** is governed by $T$ alone — *this is the two-degree-of-freedom advantage*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indirect Self Tuning Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Indirect Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator (ISTR)** is an adaptive control architecture in which the parameters of the controlled plant are first identified online using a recursive parameter estimation algorithm (most commonly **Recursive Least Squares — RLS**), and the controller parameters are subsequently recomputed at every sampling instant by solving a deterministic design equation (such as the **Diophantine equation** for minimum-variance, generalized-minimum-variance, or pole-placement design). The resulting controller is then applied to the plant under the **Certainty Equivalence Principle**, which assumes that the parameters estimated from input–output data are exact for the purpose of controller synthesis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):**
> *An indirect self-tuning regulator estimates the parameters of a parametric model of the plant on-line, and uses the estimated parameters to compute the coefficients of the controller (e.g., minimum-variance or pole-placement law) at each sampling instant. The two sub-systems are (i) a recursive identifier and (ii) an on-line control-law calculator.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Adaptive Cruise Pilot"

Imagine a **rookie pilot** flying an aircraft with shifting payload (fuel burning off, turbulence altering effective dynamics):

1. **Stage 1 — System Identification (The Pilot Watches the Gauges):**  
   The pilot constantly observes altitude, pitch, and airspeed responses to joystick commands. From these observations, he continuously *re-estimates* an internal mental model of "how this plane currently behaves." This is analogous to the **RLS identifier** tracking $\hat{\theta}(t)$.

2. **Stage 2 — Controller Re-Design (The Pilot Re-Tunes His Hands):**  
   Based on the freshly-revised mental model, the pilot immediately recalculates how much joystick deflection is needed to maintain altitude — analogous to solving the **Diophantine equation** every sample to obtain new R, S, T polynomials.

3. **Stage 3 — Certainty Equivalence Action (Apply the New Law as if It Were True):**  
   The pilot now uses these new hand-movements as though the model were perfectly accurate — this is the **certainty equivalence assumption** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Direct STR:** In a *direct* STR, the controller parameters themselves are estimated (skipping the plant-model step). In an *indirect* STR, the **plant parameters** are estimated first, and controller parameters are *derived* — hence the name "**indirect**."

## 1.3 The Certainty Equivalence Principle

The architectural foundation of every ISTR is the **Certainty Equivalence (CE) Principle**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Certainty Equivalence Principle:** *Design the controller as if the most recent parameter estimates $\hat{\theta}(t)$ were the true plant parameters $\theta$.*

In practice, this is implemented by replacing the unknown $\theta$ in the deterministic control law with its current estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ at every step. While CE is not theoretically optimal (the optimal controller would account for parameter uncertainty), it is **computationally tractable** and yields provable stability under persistent excitation and standard assumptions.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | **0.01 – 1 s** (process control) |
| Forgetting factor | $\lambda$ | **0.95 – 0.99** |
| Estimation horizon | $N_{eff}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-\lambda}$ samples |
| Minimum-variance cost | $J_{MV}$ | $E\{y^2(t+d)\}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator

The ISTR is a **closed-loop sampled-data system** with three interlocking subsystems operating simultaneously:

### 2.1.1 Subsystem A — The Plant (ARIMAX Model)

The Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) plant is described by a discrete-time **ARIMAX** (Auto-Regressive Integrated Moving Average with eXogenous input) model:

$$A(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) u(t-d) + C(z^{-1}) e(t)$$

where:
- $y(t)$ is the controlled output
- $u(t)$ is the control input  
- $e(t)$ is a zero-mean white noise sequence with variance $\sigma_e^2$
- $d \geq 1$ is the **transport delay** (integer number of samples)
- $A, B, C$ are polynomials in the backward shift operator $z^{-1}$

$$\begin{aligned}
A(z^{-1}) &= 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_A} z^{-n_A} \\
B(z^{-1}) &= b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \dots + b_{n_B} z^{-n_B} \\
C(z^{-1}) &= 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + c_2 z^{-2} + \dots + c_{n_C} z^{-n_C}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.1.2 Subsystem B — The Recursive Parameter Identifier (RLS)

The identifier maintains and updates a parameter vector estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ and a covariance matrix $P(t)$ at every sampling instant.

### 2.1.3 Subsystem C — The On-Line Controller Designer

This block receives $\hat{\theta}(t)$ and solves a deterministic design equation (e.g., the **Diophantine equation**) to compute the new controller parameter vector, which is then applied to the plant.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Two-Stage Loop"

The ISTR operates according to a strict **two-stage logic per sampling instant**:

> **Stage 1 (Identification, executed at sample $t$):**
> 1. Read current $y(t)$ and previous $u(t-1), u(t-2), \dots$
> 2. Form the regression vector $\varphi(t)$ from past $y$ and $u$ values
> 3. Compute the **prediction error** $\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1)$
> 4. Update $\hat{\theta}(t)$ and $P(t)$ using RLS formulas

> **Stage 2 (Control, executed at sample $t$):**
> 1. Use the freshly-computed $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to solve the design equation
> 2. Obtain the new controller parameter vector
> 3. Compute the control signal $u(t)$ from the current $y(t)$ and the controller law
> 4. Apply $u(t)$ to the plant (held via zero-order hold until next sample)

## 2.3 The Minimum-Variance Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator

For the special (and historically most-cited) case of **Minimum-Variance (MV)** control, the ISTR design reduces to the following structure:

**Step 1 — Diophantine Factorization:**  
Factor $C(z^{-1})$ as:

$$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d} G(z^{-1})$$

where $F(z^{-1})$ is of degree $d-1$ and $G(z^{-1})$ is of degree $n_A - 1$.

**Step 2 — Optimal MV Control Law:**

$$u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})} y(t)$$

This is the famous **self-tuning minimum-variance regulator** of Åström and Wittenmark (1973).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Notes** |
|------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|
| F1 | Plant model (ARIMAX) | $A y(t) = B u(t-d) + C e(t)$ | $d$ = integer delay |
| F2 | Regression vector | $\varphi(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_A), u(t-d), \dots, u(t-d-n_B)]^{T}$ | Length $n = n_A + n_B + 1$ |
| F3 | Parameter vector | $\theta = [a_1, \dots, a_{n_A}, b_0, \dots, b_{n_B}]^{T}$ | True (unknown) |
| F4 | RLS gain vector | $K(t) = \dfrac{P(t-1)\varphi(t)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)}$ | $n \times 1$ vector |
| F5 | Parameter update | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$ | With $\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}\hat{\theta}(t-1)$ |
| F6 | Covariance update | $P(t) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \left[ I - K(t) \varphi^{T}(t) \right] P(t-1)$ | Symmetric, $n \times n$ |
| F7 | Diophantine equation | $C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d} G(z^{-1})$ | $\deg F = d-1$, $\deg G \leq n_A - 1$ |
| F8 | MV control law | $u(t) = -\dfrac{G}{B F} y(t)$ | Implemented via RLS estimates of $A, B$ |
| F9 | Generalized MV law | $u(t) = \dfrac{G y(t) - C y^{*}(t+d)}{B F + \rho C / b_0}$ | $\rho$ = control-weighting |
| F10 | Pole-placement law | $R(z^{-1}) u(t) = T(z^{-1}) y_r(t) - S(z^{-1}) y(t)$ | $AR + z^{-d}BS = C \cdot A_m$ |
| F11 | Forgetting factor | $0 < \lambda \leq 1$ (typical **0.98**) | $\lambda < 1$ for time-varying plants |
| F12 | Cost function (MV) | $J = E\{y^2(t+d)\}$ | Minimized w.r.t. $u(t)$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Indirect self-tuning regulators are deployed in:

- **Petrochemical processes** — distillation column composition control where dynamics drift with catalyst aging
- **Paper-machine headbox control** — non-stationary pulp consistency
- **Ship-steering autopilots** — fuel load changes the ship's effective hydrodynamic model
- **Robotic manipulators with varying payloads** — inertia matrices change with grasped object mass
- **Hard-disk drive servo systems** — temperature alters track-following dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> The indirect STR is the **conceptual template** for almost all Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and Model Predictive Adaptive schemes studied later in the course.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Update Law

**Objective:** Find a recursive formula to update $\hat{\theta}(t)$ from $\hat{\theta}(t-1)$ when a new measurement $y(t)$ becomes available.

### Step 1 — Express the One-Step-Ahead Prediction

From the ARIMAX model, rearrange to predict $y(t)$ given data up to $t-1$:

$$y(t) = \varphi^{T}(t) \theta + e(t)$$

where $\varphi^{T}(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_A), u(t-d), \dots, u(t-d-n_B)]$ and $\theta = [a_1, \dots, a_{n_A}, b_0, \dots, b_{n_B}]^{T}$.

The **a priori prediction** using $\hat{\theta}(t-1)$ is:

$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \varphi^{T}(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1)$$

### Step 2 — Define the Prediction Error

$$\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1)$$

### Step 3 — State the Exponentially-Weighted Least-Squares Cost

$$J(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \theta \right]^{2}$$

The exponential weighting $\lambda^{t-k}$ with $0 < \lambda \leq 1$ exponentially forgets old data — essential for tracking time-varying plants.

### Step 4 — Minimize the Cost with Respect to $\theta$

Set $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = 0$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \varphi(k) \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \theta \right] = 0$$

Define the weighted information matrix $\Phi(t)$ and cross-correlation vector $\Psi(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi(t) &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \varphi(k) \varphi^{T}(k) \\
\Psi(t) &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \varphi(k) y(k)
\end{aligned}$$

The off-line batch solution is $\hat{\theta}(t) = \Phi^{-1}(t) \Psi(t)$.

### Step 5 — Derive the Recursive Form

Observe the rank-one update:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi(t) &= \lambda \Phi(t-1) + \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t) \\
\Psi(t) &= \lambda \Psi(t-1) + \varphi(t) y(t)
\end{aligned}$$

Using the **Matrix Inversion Lemma** $\left[ A + UCV \right]^{-1} = A^{-1} - A^{-1} U \left[ C^{-1} + V A^{-1} U \right]^{-1} V A^{-1}$ with $A = \lambda \Phi(t-1)$, $U = \varphi(t)$, $C = 1$, $V = \varphi^{T}(t)$:

$$P(t) = \Phi^{-1}(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ P(t-1) - \frac{P(t-1) \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)} \right]$$

Defining the gain vector $K(t) = \dfrac{P(t-1) \varphi(t)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)}$:

$$P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ I - K(t) \varphi^{T}(t) \right] P(t-1)$$

### Step 6 — Final Parameter Update Law

Substituting the recursive expressions:

$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \left[ y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1) \right] = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$$

This is the **RLS update law** that closes the identifier sub-system of the ISTR.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum-Variance Control Law

**Objective:** Given the ARIMAX model with $C(z^{-1}) = 1$ (white-noise disturbance), find $u(t)$ that minimizes $E\{y^2(t+d)\}$.

### Step 1 — Factor $C$ via the Diophantine Equation

$$1 = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d} G(z^{-1})$$

where $F(z^{-1}) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + \dots + f_{d-1} z^{-d+1}$ and $G(z^{-1}) = g_0 + g_1 z^{-1} + \dots + g_{n_A - 1} z^{-n_A + 1}$.

### Step 2 — Predict $y(t+d)$ Using Conditional Expectation

Multiply the plant model by $F(z^{-1})$:

$$A F \, y(t) = B F \, u(t-d) + F \, e(t)$$

Substitute $A F = 1 - z^{-d} G$:

$$y(t) - z^{-d} G \, y(t) = B F \, u(t-d) + F \, e(t)$$

Advance by $d$ steps:

$$y(t+d) = G \, y(t) + B F \, u(t) + F \, e(t+d)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Conditional Expectation

Since $e(t+d), e(t+d-1), \dots, e(t+1)$ are all independent of data at time $t$, and $F$ has no $z^{-d}$ terms:

$$E\{y(t+d) \mid t\} = G \, y(t) + B F \, u(t)$$

### Step 4 — Minimize the Mean-Square Output

The MV cost is:

$$J = E\{y^2(t+d)\} = E\left\{\left[ G y(t) + B F u(t) + F e(t+d) \right]^2 \right\}$$

The cross-term between $u(t)$-dependent part and $F e(t+d)$ vanishes (white-noise independence). Minimizing w.r.t. $u(t)$:

$$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial u(t)} = 0 \implies 2 B F \left[ G y(t) + B F u(t) \right] = 0$$

### Step 5 — The MV Control Law

$$\boxed{u(t) = -\dfrac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})} y(t)}$$

**In the ISTR**, $A$ and $B$ are replaced by their RLS estimates $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ at each step, and the Diophantine equation is re-solved numerically (e.g., by equating polynomial coefficients) to obtain $\hat{F}$ and $\hat{G}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RLS on a 2nd-Order Plant

**Problem Setup:** Consider the plant

$$y(t) = -0.8 \, y(t-1) + 0.3 \, y(t-2) + 0.5 \, u(t-1) + e(t)$$

True parameters: $a_1 = 0.8$, $a_2 = -0.3$, $b_0 = 0.5$. Initial estimates: $\hat{\theta}(0) = [0, 0, 0]^{T}$, $P(0) = 1000 \cdot I_3$, $\lambda = 0.95$.

**Sample iteration at $t = 1$** with $y(0) = y(-1) = 0$, $u(0) = 0.2$, $y(1) = 0.45$ (simulated):

Step 1 — Regression vector:

$$\varphi(1) = [-y(0), -y(-1), u(0)]^{T} = [0, 0, 0.2]^{T}$$

Step 2 — Prediction error:

$$\varepsilon(1) = y(1) - \varphi^{T}(1)\hat{\theta}(0) = 0.45 - 0 = 0.45$$

Step 3 — Gain vector:

$$K(1) = \dfrac{P(0)\varphi(1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(1) P(0) \varphi(1)} = \dfrac{1000 [0,0,0.2]^T}{0.95 + 0.2^2 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{[0, 0, 200]^T}{400.95} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.499 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 4 — Updated estimate:

$$\hat{\theta}(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.499 \end{bmatrix}(0.45) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.224 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 5 — Updated covariance:

$$P(1) = \dfrac{1}{0.95} \left[ I_3 - K(1)\varphi^{T}(1) \right] P(0) = \text{(symmetric } 3 \times 3 \text{ matrix update)}$$

**After many samples**, $\hat{\theta}(t) \to [0.8, -0.3, 0.5]^{T}$ as required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **two-stage feedback structure** of the ISTR, showing how data flows between the identifier, the controller designer, and the plant at every sampling instant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REF[Reference y_r] --> SUM1((Sum))
    YOUT[Output y] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> ERR[Tracking Error e_tr]
    ERR --> CTRL[Controller Block R S T Polynomials]
    CTRL --> USIG[Control Signal u]
    USIG --> PLANT[Plant A B C with Delay d]
    PLANT --> YOUT
    YOUT --> REGVEC[Regression Vector Builder]
    USIG --> REGVEC
    REGVEC --> PHI[Regression Vector phi]
    PHI --> RLS[Recursive Least Squares Identifier]
    RLS --> THETA[Estimated Parameters theta_hat]
    THETA --> DIO[Diophantine Equation Solver]
    DIO --> RSNEW[New R S T Polynomials]
    RSNEW --> CTRL
    NOISE[White Noise e] --> PLANT
```

## 4.2 Information-Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sampler
    participant I as RLS Identifier
    participant D as Designer Solver
    participant C as Controller Law
    participant P as Plant
    S->>P: Apply u(t) (held by ZOH)
    P-->>S: Measure y(t)
    S->>I: y(t), phi(t)
    I->>I: Update theta_hat(t) and P(t)
    I->>D: theta_hat(t)
    D->>D: Solve Diophantine AF + z^-d G = C
    D->>C: New R, S, T polynomials
    C->>C: Compute u(t+1) = -S/R y(t+1) + T/R y_r
    C-->>S: u(t+1)
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Logic of the ISTR Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INIT[Initialize theta_hat and P] --> WAIT_AWAIT[Wait for sample tick t]
    WAIT_AWAIT --> READ[Read y t and phi t]
    READ --> PRED[Compute prediction error epsilon t]
    PRED --> GAIN[Compute K t via RLS formula]
    GAIN --> UPDATE_THETA[Update theta_hat t]
    UPDATE_THETA --> UPDATE_P[Update P t]
    UPDATE_P --> DESIGN[Solve Diophantine using theta_hat]
    DESIGN --> COMPUTE_U[Compute u t from control law]
    COMPUTE_U --> APPLY[Apply u t to plant via D A or Z O H]
    APPLY --> WAIT_AWAIT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a computation, not a physical device. The arrows show the **flow of numerical data** (signals and parameter vectors) — not the flow of energy or matter. The two feedback loops visible in the architecture diagram correspond to the **regulation loop** (outer) and the **parameter adaptation loop** (inner).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator. Mention the role of the Certainty Equivalence Principle. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator (ISTR)** is an adaptive control scheme in which the parameters of the plant are identified on-line using a recursive estimation algorithm (typically RLS), and the controller parameters are then re-computed at every sampling instant by solving a deterministic design equation such as the **Diophantine equation**. The role of the **Certainty Equivalence Principle** is to permit the use of the estimated parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ as if they were the true plant parameters $\theta$ when computing the control law, thereby decoupling the design equation from the estimation error statistics.

---

### Q2. State the Diophantine equation used in minimum-variance self-tuning control. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a plant $A(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) u(t-d) + C(z^{-1}) e(t)$, the Diophantine equation is:

$$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d} G(z^{-1})$$

where $F(z^{-1})$ has degree $d-1$ and $G(z^{-1})$ has degree at most $n_A - 1$. The minimum-variance control law derived from it is $u(t) = -\dfrac{G}{B F} \, y(t)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts — typically (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. The model solutions below include full step-by-step working.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) update equations used in the identifier of an indirect self-tuning regulator. Clearly state the role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plant as a linear regression:**  
From $A y(t) = B u(t-d) + e(t)$ (with $C = 1$ for simplicity), write $y(t) = \varphi^{T}(t)\theta + e(t)$, where $\varphi(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_A), u(t-d), \dots]^{T}$ and $\theta = [a_1, \dots, b_0, \dots]^{T}$.

**Step 2 — Exponentially weighted LS cost:**  
$$J(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \theta \right]^2$$
With $0 < \lambda \leq 1$, old samples are exponentially down-weighted, allowing the estimator to **track time-varying** plant parameters. Typical value: $\lambda = 0.98$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Minimization:**  
$\partial J / \partial \theta = 0$ gives the batch solution $\hat{\theta}(t) = \Phi^{-1}(t) \Psi(t)$ where  
$\Phi(t) = \lambda \Phi(t-1) + \varphi(t)\varphi^{T}(t)$ and $\Psi(t) = \lambda \Psi(t-1) + \varphi(t) y(t)$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Apply the Matrix Inversion Lemma to obtain the recursive covariance update:**  
$$P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[ I - \frac{P(t-1)\varphi(t)\varphi^{T}(t)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)} \right] P(t-1)$$

**Step 5 — Define gain vector and parameter update:**  
$$K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\varphi(t)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)} \qquad \hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \left[ y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1) \right]$$ **(3 marks)**

**Role of $\lambda$:** $\lambda$ trades off **noise smoothing** ($\lambda \to 1$) against **adaptation speed** ($\lambda \to 0.9$ or less). For constant plants, set $\lambda = 1$; for time-varying plants, choose $0.95 \leq \lambda \leq 0.99$.

---

**(b) For the plant $y(t) = 1.5 y(t-1) - 0.7 y(t-2) + 0.4 u(t-1) + 0.2 u(t-2) + e(t)$ design a minimum-variance indirect self-tuning regulator. State the Diophantine equation, solve it, and write the control law. `[7 marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the polynomials:**  
$A = 1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}$, $B = 0.4 + 0.2 z^{-1}$, $C = 1$, $d = 1$.

**Step 2 — State the Diophantine equation with $d = 1$:**  
Since $d = 1$, $F(z^{-1})$ has degree $d - 1 = 0$, i.e., $F = 1$. The equation becomes:  
$$1 = A \cdot 1 + z^{-1} G \implies G(z^{-1}) = z(1 - A) = 1.5 - 0.7 z^{-1}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Form the MV control law:**  
$$u(t) = -\frac{G}{B F} y(t) = -\frac{1.5 - 0.7 z^{-1}}{0.4 + 0.2 z^{-1}} y(t)$$

**Step 4 — Express as a difference equation:**  
$(0.4 + 0.2 z^{-1}) u(t) = -(1.5 - 0.7 z^{-1}) y(t)$  
$\implies 0.4 u(t) + 0.2 u(t-1) = -1.5 y(t) + 0.7 y(t-1)$  
$\implies u(t) = -3.75 y(t) + 1.75 y(t-1) - 0.5 u(t-1)$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Indirect STR modification:**  
In practice, the coefficients $1.5, 0.7, 0.4, 0.2$ are **replaced at every step** by the RLS estimates $\hat{a}_1(t), \hat{a}_2(t), \hat{b}_0(t), \hat{b}_1(t)$ and the Diophantine equation is re-solved. **(2 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Explain the architecture of an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator with a neat block diagram. Distinguish between the parameter estimation loop and the control loop. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** An ISTR consists of three interconnected blocks: (i) a **recursive parameter identifier** (RLS), (ii) a **control-law computer** (Diophantine solver), and (iii) the **plant** with its ZOH and sampler. **(2 marks)**

**Block Diagram (described in words):**  
- The plant output $y(t)$ is fed back, sampled, and used by both the **regulator** (to compute the next $u(t)$) and the **identifier** (to form $\varphi(t)$ and update $\hat{\theta}(t)$).  
- The identifier passes $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to the **designer**, which solves the Diophantine equation and updates the controller polynomials.  
- The controller then uses the new polynomials to compute the next $u(t)$. **(2 marks)**

**Distinction between loops:**

| Feature | **Control Loop (Outer)** | **Estimation Loop (Inner)** |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Sample-rate; modulates $u(t)$ | Sample-rate; updates $\hat{\theta}(t)$ |
| Goal | Tracks reference $y_r(t)$ | Tracks true $\theta$ |
| Signals | $y(t), y_r(t), u(t)$ | $\varphi(t), \varepsilon(t), \hat{\theta}(t)$ |
| Convergence | Stabilized by feedback | Driven by persistent excitation | **(3 marks)** |

---

**(b) Discuss the implementation issues, advantages, and limitations of the Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Implementation Issues:**  
1. **Numerical stability of $P(t)$:** The covariance update can lose positive-definiteness due to round-off. Fix: use **UD-factorization** or **square-root RLS**. **(1.5 marks)**  
2. **Bursting phenomenon:** When $\lambda < 1$ and excitation is poor, $P(t)$ grows without bound, causing occasional large parameter jumps and output bursts. Fix: enforce a **covariance reset** or use **variable forgetting factor**. **(1.5 marks)**  
3. **Controller admissibility:** Estimates may yield unstable $B(z^{-1})$ roots (unstable pole-zero cancellation). Fix: monitor closed-loop stability with **projections** or **dead zones**. **(1 mark)**

**Advantages:**  
1. **Conceptual simplicity** — separates identification from design.  
2. **Versatility** — can implement MV, GMV, pole-placement, or LQG by changing only the design equation.  
3. **Tunable** — designer has full control over the cost function and weighting. **(1.5 marks)**

**Limitations:**  
1. **Computational load** — must solve a polynomial Diophantine equation every sample.  
2. **Sensitivity to estimate quality** — poor early estimates yield poor transient control.  
3. **No inherent stability proof under CE** — global stability requires additional assumptions (e.g., persistent excitation, SPR condition). **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in ISTR Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the regression-vector construction** — Many students write the RLS update but omit the explicit form of $\varphi(t)$, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Diophantine equation** — The equation is $C = AF + z^{-d}G$, **not** $C = AF + z^{-d}G$ with reversed sign; check $A, B$ coefficients carefully.
> 3. **Confusing Direct and Indirect STR** — A common error is to write the controller parameter estimator for a *direct* STR when the question asks for an *indirect* one. Always re-derive the plant model first.
> 4. **No initial-condition discussion** — Examiners expect $P(0) = \sigma_0 I$ with $\sigma_0$ large (e.g., $10^3$ to $10^6 I$) and $\hat{\theta}(0) = 0$ unless prior knowledge is given.
> 5. **Skipping the certainty equivalence justification** — When asked "why indirect?", many omit the statement: *"Because we use $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ in place of $A, B$ in the deterministic control law."*
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — Persistent excitation, bounded delay $d$, minimum-phase plant (for MV) — all carry marks if mentioned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Indirect Self-Tuning Regulators**

- [x] **ISTR = Identification (RLS) + On-line Design (Diophantine) + Certainty Equivalence Action**
- [x] **Plant model:** $A y(t) = B u(t-d) + C e(t)$ — always confirm the delay $d$ explicitly
- [x] **Regression vector $\varphi(t)$** uses **past** $y$ and **past** $u(t-d)$, never the present $u(t)$
- [x] **RLS gain** $K(t) = \dfrac{P(t-1)\varphi(t)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)}$
- [x] **Parameter update** $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\varepsilon(t)$
- [x] **Covariance update** $P(t) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[ I - K(t)\varphi^{T}(t) \right] P(t-1)$
- [x] **Diophantine equation:** $C = AF + z^{-d}G$, with $\deg F = d - 1$
- [x] **MV control law:** $u(t) = -\dfrac{G}{BF} y(t)$
- [x] **Certainty Equivalence:** substitute $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ for $A, B$ in the control law
- [x] **Forgetting factor** $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ — typically 0.95 to 0.99 for slow drift
- [x] **Initialization:** $P(0) = \alpha I$ with $\alpha$ large; $\hat{\theta}(0) = 0$ (or a priori guess)
- [x] **Key dangers:** bursting, numerical drift, non-minimum-phase zeros, lack of persistent excitation
- [x] **Stability requires:** persistent excitation, bounded delay, and the plant to be minimum-phase (for MV)
- [x] **Distinguish:** Direct STR estimates controller parameters; Indirect STR estimates **plant** parameters
- [x] **Applications:** petrochemicals, paper machines, ship steering, robotic manipulators, HDD servos

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Time Self Tuners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Time Self Tuners ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Continuous Time Self Tuner (CTST):** An adaptive control system in which the controller parameters are updated **continuously in real time** (as opposed to at discrete sampling instants) based on the on-line estimation of the unknown or time-varying plant parameters, such that the closed-loop system meets a prespecified performance criterion. A Continuous Time Self Tuner is a closed-loop adaptive control architecture consisting of two coupled subsystems operating in the **continuous time domain** $t \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$: 1. **A recursive parameter estimator** that identifies the unknown plant parameters in real time using gradient-based or Lyapunov-based update laws. 2. **A controller synthesis block** that uses the estimated parameters to compute the control signal $u(t)$ at every instant. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Continuous-time self tuners are distinguished from their discrete-time counterparts by the absence of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) and a sampler; both estimation and control laws evolve as **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** rather than difference equations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Smart Cruise Control:** Imagine driving a car whose weight changes drastically every few minutes (passengers getting in and out, cargo loaded/unloaded). A *fixed-gain* cruise controller would be either too sluggish or too jerky. A **continuous-time self tuner** is like a smart driver who **continuously** feels the acceleration response, immediately re-estimates the car's mass, and instantly adjusts the gas-pedal pressure — all **smoothly and without any discrete sampling pauses**. The estimation and control adjustments happen in a fluid, continuous manner, exactly mimicking a human's analog reflex action. ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Plant output | $y(t)$ | Measured continuous response | | Control input | $u(t)$ | Continuous actuator signal | | Parameter vector | $\theta(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Time-varying estimated parameters | | Regressor vector | $\phi(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Vector of measurable signals | | Adaptation gain | $\gamma > 0$ | Tuning knob for learning rate | | Tracking / regulation error | $e(t)$ | $y_m(t) - y(t)$ or $r(t) - y(t)$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** In a self tuner, the control law is **re-computed** (re-tuned) at every instant using a parameter estimator. This is fundamentally different from a *gain-scheduling* system where the gain is pre-tabulated offline. ### 1.4 Classification of Continuous Time Self Tuners ``` Continuous Time Self Tuners │ ├── 1. Direct Self Tuners │ └── Controller parameters updated directly │ to minimize a cost/ error index. │ └── 2. Indirect Self Tuners └── Plant parameters estimated → controller re-designed (e.g., pole-placement, LQR) using certainty equivalence principle. ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 General Architecture of a Continuous Time Self Tuner A CTST has the following interacting blocks (operating simultaneously, continuously): 1. **Plant** $\mathcal{P}$: Unknown system, possibly time-varying. 2. **Identifier / Estimator**: Produces $\hat{\theta}(t)$ from $\{u(\tau), y(\tau)\}$ for $\tau \leq t$. 3. **Controller Redesign Block**: Computes controller parameters $\hat{\Theta}(t) = f(\hat{\theta}(t))$. 4. **Controller**: Generates $u(t) = \hat{\Theta}^T(t)\,\Phi(t)$. 5. **Reference Model** (optional, in MRAC-type direct self tuners): Defines desired response $y_m(t)$. > [!NOTE] > **Certainty Equivalence Principle (CEP):** The controller at every instant is designed as though the current estimates $\hat{\theta}(t)$ were the true parameters. This is the cornerstone of indirect self tuning. CEP makes analysis tractable but does **not** guarantee stability — this is the famous "self-tuning regulator paradox" addressed by multiple KTU questions. ### 2.2 Parameter Estimation in Continuous Time The plant is modeled linearly in the parameters: $$y(t) = \theta^{*T}\,\phi(t) + \eta(t)$$ where $\theta^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the true (unknown) parameter vector, $\phi(t)$ is the regressor, and $\eta(t)$ represents disturbance/ modelling error. **Continuous-Time Gradient (LMS-type) Update Law:** The standard **continuous-time least mean square (LMS)** estimator is given by: $$\boxed{\;\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma\, \phi(t)\, \varepsilon(t)\;}$$ where: - $\Gamma = \gamma I_n$, with adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$ - $\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t)\phi(t)$ is the *a posteriori* prediction error. ### 2.3 The MIT Rule — The Heart of Direct Self Tuners > [!IMPORTANT] > **MIT Rule Definition:** The MIT rule, originally proposed at the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory, adjusts parameters in the direction of the **negative gradient** of a chosen cost function $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$. If $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2(\theta,t)$, the MIT rule is: $$\boxed{\;\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}\;}$$ **Interpretation of each term:** | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | $e(\theta,t)$ | Performance error (output deviation) | | $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | *Sensitivity derivative* — how the error changes with $\theta$ | | $\gamma$ | Adaptation gain (small for slow adaptation, large for fast but possibly unstable) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Many students write $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ **without** the error $e$ multiplier. The MIT rule is **proportional to the error AND its sensitivity derivative**. This is a frequently penalized mistake in board exams. ### 2.4 Stability Issue & Modified MIT Rule The pure MIT rule can lead to **instability** for large $\gamma$ or when the plant is non-minimum phase. A **normalised / modified MIT rule** is used: $$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma\, \frac{e\, (\partial e/\partial \theta)}{1 + \alpha\, \phi^T \phi}, \quad \alpha > 0$$ The denominator $\alpha\,\phi^T\phi$ prevents parameter drift when the regressor energy is small (a phenomenon called *parameter drift / bursting*). ### 2.5 Direct vs Indirect — Comparative Analysis | Aspect | Direct Self Tuner | Indirect Self Tuner | |--------|-------------------|----------------------| | **Estimated quantity** | Controller parameters $\Theta$ directly | Plant parameters $\theta$ | | **Design step** | Single (gradient update) | Two (estimate + re-design) | | **Computation per step** | Low | Higher (involves solving Riccati / Diophantine) | | **Insight** | Less interpretable | More insight into plant behaviour | | **Robustness** | Often more robust when model is rough | Sensitive to estimation errors | | **Example** | MIT-rule MRAC | Pole-placement STR | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma\,\phi\,\varepsilon$ | Continuous LMS estimator | | 2 | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | MIT rule | | 3 | $u(t) = \hat{\theta}_1(t)\, r(t) - \hat{\theta}_2(t)\, y(t)$ | Direct adaptive control law | | 4 | $\varepsilon(t) = y - \hat{\theta}^T \phi$ | Estimation / prediction error | | 5 | $\dot{V} = -e^2 \leq 0$ | Lyapunov stability (designer) | | 6 | $\dfrac{\dot{\hat{\theta}}_i}{\theta_i} = \dfrac{-\gamma\, e\, s_i}{1+\alpha\,\phi^T\phi}$ | Normalised update (avoids drift) | | 7 | $\sigma$-modification: $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma e s - \sigma \hat{\theta}$ | Robustness to bounded disturbances | | 8 | $V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma^{-1}\tilde{\theta}^T\tilde{\theta}$ | Standard Lyapunov function | > [!TIP] > **Units / Magnitudes for KTU Board Problems:** Adaptation gain $\gamma$ is dimensionless but typically chosen in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{2}$ depending on the time-constants of the plant. A higher $\gamma$ gives faster adaptation but a smaller stability margin. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Aerospace flight control** with changing aerodynamics (Mach number variation). - **Process control** in chemical reactors where reaction kinetics drift. - **Robotic manipulator control** with varying payload inertia. - **Automotive active suspension** and engine control units. - **High-precision CNC machines** with tool wear compensation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous-Time LMS (Gradient) Estimator **Starting point:** Consider a plant linear in unknown parameters: $$y(t) = \theta^{*T} \phi(t) + \eta(t)$$ with $\theta^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ unknown and constant (or slowly varying). The **prediction** of the output using the current estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ is: $$\hat{y}(t) = \hat{\theta}^T(t)\,\phi(t)$$ Define the prediction error: $$\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t)\phi(t)$$ Define the **instantaneous squared error cost**: $$J(\hat{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon^2(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[y - \hat{\theta}^T\phi\right]^2$$ Take the gradient w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}} &= \varepsilon \cdot \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial \hat{\theta}} \\ &= \varepsilon \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{\theta}}\bigl[y - \hat{\theta}^T\phi\bigr] \\ &= \varepsilon \cdot (-\phi) \\ &= -\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t) \end{aligned} $$ The **steepest-descent continuous update** is: $$ \boxed{\;\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma\,\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = \Gamma\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)\;} $$ A sign convention in many textbooks (so that $\hat{\theta}$ chases $y$) is: $$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\gamma\, \phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)$$ with $\gamma > 0$. This is the celebrated **continuous-time LMS / gradient estimator**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the MIT Rule **Setup:** Suppose the closed-loop system has a scalar tunable parameter $\theta$, and let the error between the desired response $y_m$ and the actual response $y$ be: $$e(\theta,t) = y(\theta,t) - y_m(t)$$ Define the cost: $$J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^2(\theta,t)$$ To minimize $J$, perform gradient descent in the $\theta$-direction. Since $\theta$ is the parameter being tuned, its time-derivative follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{\theta} &= -\gamma\, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} \\ &= -\gamma \cdot e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \quad \text{(chain rule: } \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}\text{)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{\theta}(t) = -\gamma\, e(t)\, \frac{\partial e(\theta,t)}{\partial \theta}\;} $$ The partial derivative $\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ is called the **sensitivity function** $s(t) = \partial e / \partial \theta$, which is obtained by differentiating the plant model with respect to $\theta$ and interchanging the order of differentiation. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example — MIT Rule on a First-Order Plant > **Plant:** $\;\dot{y} = -a\,y + b\,u$, with $a$ known, $b$ unknown. > **Reference model:** $\;\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r(t)$. > **Control law:** $\;u(t) = \theta(t)\, r(t)$ (we will tune $\theta$ so that $b\,\theta \to b_m$). > **Goal:** Derive the MIT update law. **Step 1 — Express the closed-loop error:** $$ \dot{y} = -a\,y + b\,\theta\, r(t) $$ Subtract reference model: $$ \dot{e} = \dot{y} - \dot{y}_m = -a\,y + b\,\theta\, r(t) + a_m y_m - b_m r $$ Assuming perfect model matching requires $a = a_m$ and $b\theta = b_m$, the error dynamics simplify to: $$ \dot{e} = -a\, e + (b\theta - b_m)\, r(t) $$ **Step 2 — Compute sensitivity $\partial e / \partial \theta$:** Treating $b\theta$ as a product and assuming $e$ is differentiable in $\theta$: $$ \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = b \int_0^t e^{-a(t-\tau)} r(\tau)\, d\tau $$ For a slowly varying $\theta$ (quasi-static assumption), the standard simplification used in the MIT rule is: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \approx -b \int_0^t e^{-a(t-\tau)} r(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; -\frac{b}{a}\, r(t)\;} $$ (the last approximation holds when $r$ is slowly varying relative to the plant pole $a$). **Step 3 — MIT update law:** $$ \dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e \cdot \left(-\frac{b}{a} r\right) = \frac{\gamma\, b}{a}\, e\, r $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{\theta}(t) = \kappa\, e(t)\, r(t), \quad \kappa = \dfrac{\gamma\, b}{a}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Closed-form solution sketch (constant $r = r_0$):** With $r(t) = r_0$ and assuming $b$ is approximately absorbed into $\kappa$, the linearised error dynamics are: $$ \dot{e} = -a\,e + \kappa\, b\, r_0 \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau - b_m r_0 $$ A first-order approximation yields exponential convergence of $e \to 0$ when $\kappa b r_0 / a < 1$ (stability condition), and $\theta \to b_m / b$. --- ### 3.4 Lyapunov-Based Update Law (Alternative to MIT) A more rigorous derivation uses the Lyapunov function: $$V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}^T \tilde{\theta}$$ where $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta^*$. Differentiating along trajectories: $$ \dot{V} = e\, \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}^T \dot{\hat{\theta}} $$ To make $\dot{V} \leq 0$, choose: $$ \dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma\, e\, \phi(t) $$ Then $\dot{V} = -a\, e^2 \leq 0$ (assuming a stable error dynamics $\dot{e} = -a e + \phi^T \tilde{\theta}$), guaranteeing stability in the sense of Lyapunov. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The MIT rule is **heuristic** (no formal stability proof), while the Lyapunov-based update is **formally derived** to ensure $\dot{V} \leq 0$. Both produce the same algebraic form, but the latter is what examiners look for when asked about *stable adaptive laws*. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Scenario | Recommended Estimator | Reason | |----------|----------------------|--------| | Slow parameter drift, no disturbance | MIT rule | Simplicity, low computation | | Fast parameter variation | Normalised MIT / LMS | Avoids parameter drift | | Unmodelled dynamics present | $\sigma$-modification or $e$-modification | Robustness | | Strict stability required | Lyapunov-based update | Formal proof of stability | | Plant has time-varying DC gain | Recursive Least Squares (RLS) variant | Exponential convergence | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Indirect Continuous Time Self Tuner ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference r(t)"] --> SUMA(("+")) SUMA --> CTRL["Controller
u = f(theta_hat)"] CTRL --> PLANT["Plant
unknown theta*"] PLANT --> OUT["Output y(t)"] OUT --> SUMA OUT --> ID["Identifier
(Estimator)"] CTRL -. "u(t)" .-> ID ID --> THETA["Estimated parameters
theta_hat(t)"] THETA --> CTRL THETA --> NOTE["Certainty
Equivalence"] style PLANT fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style ID fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366 style CTRL fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#330066 style THETA fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The plant is the only block with **unknown** internal parameters. - The identifier observes $u(t)$ and $y(t)$ continuously and updates $\hat{\theta}(t)$. - The controller block is *itself* an output of the identifier — making the entire loop **adaptive**. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Direct Continuous Time Self Tuner (MIT Rule) ```mermaid flowchart LR RM["Reference Model
y_m(t)"] --> SUB(("−")) Y["Plant output y(t)"] --> SUB SUB --> E["Error e(t)"] E --> MIT["MIT Update Block
dtheta/dt = -gamma * e * (de/dtheta)"] SENS["Sensitivity
(de/dtheta)"] --> MIT MIT --> THETA["theta_hat(t)"] THETA --> CTRL["Controller
u = theta_hat * phi"] CTRL --> PLANT["Plant"] PLANT --> Y style RM fill:#ffd699 style MIT fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style PLANT fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Direct vs Indirect Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DIRECT["DIRECT SELF TUNER"] D1["u, y"] --> D2["Compute e(t) = y - y_m"] D2 --> D3["Update controller parameters
via MIT/Lyapunov"] D3 --> D4["Apply u = Theta_hat^T * phi"] end subgraph INDIRECT["INDIRECT SELF TUNER"] I1["u, y"] --> I2["Estimate plant parameters
theta_hat (LMS)"] I2 --> I3["Re-design controller
(pole-placement, LQR)"] I3 --> I4["Apply new controller"] end style DIRECT fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style INDIRECT fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600 ``` ### 4.4 Signal-Flow Diagram of the MIT Update Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A["theta(t)"] --> B["Plant + Controller
(closed loop)"] B --> C["y(t)"] C --> D["Comparator: e = y - y_m"] RM["y_m(t)"] --> D D --> E["MIT Block:
-gamma * e * de/dtheta"] E --> F["Integrator: dot(theta)"] F --> A style E fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000 style F fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note on Diagrams:** In KTU board exams, students are expected to draw **block diagrams** showing the signal flow, the *error extraction point*, the *adaptation mechanism*, and the *feedback path to the parameter*. Mermaid-style descriptions are accepted only as supplementary aids; the examiner will look for hand-drawn boxes, summing junctions, and arrow labels in the answer sheet. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adaptive Control]` **Differentiate between a direct and an indirect continuous-time self tuner.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Direct Self Tuner | Indirect Self Tuner | |--------|-------------------|----------------------| | Estimated quantity | Controller parameters | Plant parameters | | Update mechanism | Gradient/MIT rule on a cost in terms of controller parameters | Gradient/Lyapunov rule on plant parameters, then re-design | | Computational cost | Low | High (requires on-line redesign) | | Principle used | Heuristic (MIT) or Lyapunov | Certainty equivalence | *(1 mark for the table headings/category; 1 mark for direct definition; 1 mark for indirect definition.)* --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adaptive Control]` **State the MIT rule and explain the role of the sensitivity derivative $\partial e / \partial \theta$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The MIT rule for a scalar tunable parameter $\theta$ is: $$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\gamma\, e(t)\, \frac{\partial e(\theta,t)}{\partial \theta}$$ where $e$ is the performance error and $\gamma$ is the adaptation gain. The sensitivity derivative $\partial e/\partial \theta$ indicates **how the error would change for an infinitesimal change in the parameter** — it is the gradient direction along which $\theta$ must move to reduce the error. *(1 mark for the rule, 1 mark for the sensitivity term, 1 mark for its physical/role interpretation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the MIT rule for a self-tuning regulator starting from the cost function $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2$. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A first-order plant is given by $\dot{y} = -2y + b\,u$, where $b$ is unknown. A reference model is $\dot{y}_m = -2y_m + 4r(t)$. Using the MIT rule with control law $u = \theta r(t)$, derive the update law for $\theta$ and discuss the conditions for stability. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — Derivation of the MIT Rule (7 marks):** *Step 1 (1 mark):* Define the closed-loop error as $e(\theta,t) = y(\theta,t) - y_m(t)$, where $y$ depends on $\theta$ through the control law. *Step 2 (1 mark):* Define the cost $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^2(\theta,t)$. *Step 3 (1 mark):* Compute the gradient: $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$ *Step 4 (1 mark):* Apply continuous-time steepest descent to minimize $J$: $$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$$ *Step 5 (1 mark):* Substitute the gradient: $$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$ *Step 6 (1 mark):* Define the sensitivity function $s(t) = \partial e/\partial \theta$ and note that it is obtained by differentiating the plant equation w.r.t. $\theta$ (sensitivity model). *Step 7 (1 mark):* The MIT rule is then $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, s(t)$, which adjusts $\theta$ in the direction that reduces the squared error. --- **(b) — Application (7 marks):** *Step 1 (1 mark):* The control law is $u = \theta r(t)$, and the closed-loop system becomes: $$\dot{y} = -2y + b\,\theta\, r(t)$$ *Step 2 (1 mark):* Subtract the reference model: $$\dot{e} = \dot{y} - \dot{y}_m = -2e + (b\theta - 4)\,r(t)$$ since $a = a_m = 2$. *Step 3 (1 mark):* Sensitivity derivative: Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$ (with $b$ treated as known/approximated): $$\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -b \int_0^t e^{-2(t-\tau)} r(\tau)\, d\tau$$ *Step 4 (1 mark):* Quasi-static approximation (assume $r$ varies slowly): $$\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \approx -\frac{b}{2}\, r(t)$$ *Step 5 (1 mark):* Apply the MIT rule: $$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e \cdot \left(-\frac{b}{2} r\right) = \frac{\gamma b}{2}\, e\, r(t)$$ So $\kappa = \gamma b/2$. *Step 6 (1 mark):* Closed-loop analysis — substitute back into the error equation. Using a small-signal linearisation around $e=0, \theta = \theta^* = 4/b$: $$\dot{e} = -2e + \frac{b^2 \gamma}{2} r_0^2 \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau - 4r_0 + b\theta_0 r_0$$ Stability requires the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2 - \tfrac{b^2\gamma r_0^2}{2s}}$ to be Hurwitz. *Step 7 (1 mark):* The condition is approximately $\gamma b^2 r_0^2 < 4$ for stability, and $\theta$ converges to $4/b$ exponentially. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of an indirect continuous-time self tuner. State the Certainty Equivalence Principle and discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A plant is described by $y(t) = a\,y(t) + b\,u(t)$ with unknown parameters. Using the continuous-time LMS estimator, derive the update laws for the estimates $\hat{a}(t)$ and $\hat{b}(t)$ if the regressor is $\phi(t) = [y(t),\; u(t)]^T$. State the stability condition. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — Indirect Self Tuner Architecture (7 marks):** *Step 1 (2 marks):* Block diagram (described in words; refer to Section 4.1): The architecture has three coupled blocks: **Plant**, **Identifier**, and **Controller Redesign + Controller**. The reference input $r(t)$ enters the controller, the output $y(t)$ is fed back, and the identifier observes $u(t)$ and $y(t)$ to update $\hat{\theta}(t)$. The controller re-design block computes new controller parameters $\hat{\Theta}(t) = f(\hat{\theta}(t))$. *Step 2 (2 marks):* Certainty Equivalence Principle (CEP): *"At every instant, design the controller as if the current estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ were the true parameter $\theta^*$."* *Step 3 (1 mark):* CEP is attractive because it decouples estimation and control design. *Step 4 (1 mark):* Limitations of CEP: - It provides no formal stability guarantee. - During the transient (before $\hat{\theta} \to \theta^*$), the controller may be detuned. - It is not robust to unmodelled dynamics. *Step 5 (1 mark):* Mention the need for persistent excitation (PE) of the regressor to guarantee convergence of $\hat{\theta}$. --- **(b) — LMS Estimator Derivation (7 marks):** *Step 1 (1 mark):* Plant model: $y = a y + b u + \eta$ (dropping explicit $t$ for brevity). *Step 2 (1 mark):* Regressor: $\phi = [y, u]^T$, true parameter: $\theta^* = [a, b]^T$. *Step 3 (1 mark):* Prediction: $\hat{y} = \hat{a} y + \hat{b} u = \hat{\theta}^T \phi$. *Step 4 (1 mark):* Prediction error: $\varepsilon = y - \hat{y}$. *Step 5 (2 marks):* LMS update law: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\hat{a}} \\ \dot{\hat{b}} \end{bmatrix} = -\gamma \begin{bmatrix} y \\ u \end{bmatrix} \bigl(y - \hat{a} y - \hat{b} u\bigr) $$ i.e., $\dot{\hat{a}} = -\gamma y \varepsilon$ and $\dot{\hat{b}} = -\gamma u \varepsilon$. *Step 6 (1 mark):* Stability condition (Lyapunov): using $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\tilde{\theta}^T \tilde{\theta}$ with $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta^*$, we obtain $\dot{V} = -\gamma \varepsilon^2 \leq 0$, hence the estimates are bounded and the error is non-increasing. **Persistent excitation** of $\phi$ is required for $\hat{\theta} \to \theta^*$ exponentially. *Step 7 (1 mark):* Sufficient condition: $\int_t^{t+T} \phi(\tau)\phi^T(\tau)\,d\tau \geq \delta I$ for some $\delta, T > 0$ (PE condition). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CTST Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the error $e$ in the MIT rule** — students often write $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, \partial e/\partial \theta$. Always multiply by $e$. (Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.) > 2. **Confusing parameter drift with instability** — the parameters can drift even when the output error is small. The solution is $\sigma$-modification or normalisation. > 3. **Not mentioning the Certainty Equivalence Principle** in indirect self-tuner questions — examiners allocate at least **2 marks** for this concept. > 4. **Confusing the regressor $\phi$ with the parameter vector $\theta$** — they live in dual spaces; $\phi$ is measurable, $\theta$ is estimated. > 5. **Skipping the quasi-static assumption** in MIT-rule derivations — examiners want to see the statement "assuming slow variation of $\theta$ and the regressor, the sensitivity derivative is approximated as a static function". > 6. **Forgetting the persistent excitation (PE) condition** — for any convergence proof, PE is essential; its absence is a **1-mark deduction**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Time Self Tuners** - ✅ A **Continuous Time Self Tuner** re-tunes its controller **continuously** (ODE-based) using real-time parameter estimates. - ✅ **Two flavours**: *Direct* (tune controller params) and *Indirect* (estimate plant params → redesign). - ✅ **Core update law (LMS)**: $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma\, \phi\, \varepsilon$. - ✅ **MIT Rule**: $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, (\partial e/\partial \theta)$. Remember the **$e$ factor**. - ✅ **Sensitivity function** $s(t) = \partial e/\partial \theta$ is computed by differentiating the plant model w.r.t. $\theta$. - ✅ **Certainty Equivalence Principle (CEP)** is the *assumption* underlying indirect self tuners. - ✅ **Parameter drift** is mitigated by **$\sigma$-modification** or **normalisation** ($\alpha \phi^T \phi$ in the denominator). - ✅ **Stability proof** uses the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^T \tilde{\theta}$. - ✅ **Persistent excitation (PE)** of the regressor $\phi(t)$ is necessary for parameter convergence. - ✅ **Application domains**: aerospace, process control, robotics, automotive systems. - ✅ **Direct self tuners** are computationally lighter; **indirect self tuners** are more transparent. - ✅ **MIT rule is heuristic** (no formal stability guarantee); **Lyapunov-based update is formal** — same form, different derivation. - ✅ For **first-order plants** with single tunable gain, MIT rule reduces to $\dot{\theta} = \kappa\, e\, r(t)$ with $\kappa = \gamma b/a$.

topics.title: Direct Self Tuning Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Direct Self-Tuning Regulator (DSTR)** is an adaptive control architecture in which the **controller parameters are estimated directly** from observed input–output data, bypassing the intermediate step of explicitly identifying the physical plant model. The estimated controller parameters are subsequently used, via the *certainty equivalence principle*, to update the control law in real time.

Mathematically, given a plant model parameterized by an unknown vector **θ**, the indirect approach first estimates **θ** and then solves a design equation (e.g., Diophantine equation) to obtain the controller parameters **ρ**. In contrast, the direct approach re-parameterizes the regulator so that the *same parameters that appear in the minimum-variance or one-step-ahead control law* are themselves estimated directly from data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Direct self-tuning regulators are a cornerstone of *implicit adaptive control*, where the adaptive loop closes on the **controller parameters**, not the plant parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on an unfamiliar road where the friction coefficient changes between dry asphalt, wet leaves, and ice.

- **Indirect (Explicit) Self-Tuning:** You first *measure* the road friction using a sensor (plant identification), then *calculate* how much to turn the steering wheel (controller design).
- **Direct (Implicit) Self-Tuning:** You skip the friction measurement. Based on how the car *actually* responded to your previous steering inputs (observed I/O data), you *directly adjust* how much to turn the wheel this instant. Your brain is the recursive estimator working directly on steering commands.

The key insight: **why estimate friction if all you ultimately need is the steering correction?**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sampling period:** $T_s$ (seconds) — must satisfy Shannon–Nyquist: $T_s < \pi / \omega_{max}$
- **Forgetting factor:** $\lambda \in (0.95, 1.0)$ — typically **$\lambda = 0.98$** for slowly time-varying plants
- **Adaptive gain initial covariance:** $P(0) = \alpha I$ where $\alpha \in [100, 1000]$
- **Closed-loop bandwidth:** $\omega_c$ constrained by $T_s \cdot \omega_c \leq 0.1$ for robust stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Certainty Equivalence Principle:** Replace the unknown true parameters in the optimal control law with their current estimates, as if they were the true values. This is the *design glue* that links estimation and control in any self-tuner.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Direct Self-Tuning Regulator

The Direct STR operates through three sequential stages, each executed at every sampling instant $k$:

1. **Plant Re-parameterization:** The plant model is algebraically manipulated so that the parameters of the *minimum-variance controller* appear explicitly in the prediction equation. This is the defining algebraic trick of DSTR.
2. **Recursive Parameter Estimation:** A Recursive Least Squares (RLS) or Recursive Extended Least Squares (RELS) algorithm estimates the controller parameters online from the innovation $\epsilon(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k)$.
3. **Certainty-Equivalence Control Update:** The estimated controller parameters are plugged into the control law, generating $u(k)$ for the next instant.

## 2.2 The Core Idea: Re-parameterization

Consider a CARMA plant model:

$$A(q^{-1}) y(k) = B(q^{-1}) u(k-d) + C(q^{-1}) e(k)$$

The minimum-variance controller (Aström's classical result) requires solving the *Diophantine equation*:

$$C(q^{-1}) = A(q^{-1}) F(q^{-1}) + q^{-d} G(q^{-1})$$

with control law:

$$u(k) = \frac{G(q^{-1})}{B(q^{-1}) F(q^{-1})} y^*(k+d) - \frac{F(q^{-1})}{B(q^{-1})} y(k)$$

The direct approach re-writes the **d-step-ahead predictor** so that the controller coefficients $\{g_0, g_1, \ldots, g_{n-1}\}$ and $\{f_0, f_1, \ldots, f_{d-1}\}$ appear as the *regression vector*. No plant parameters $a_i, b_i$ ever appear in the estimator.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plant CARMA | $A y(k) = B u(k-d) + C e(k)$ | $q^{-1}$ is the backward shift operator |
| 2 | Minimum cost index | $J = E\{[y(k+d) - y^*(k+d)]^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_k\}$ | $\mathcal{F}_k$ is information up to time $k$ |
| 3 | d-step ahead predictor | $\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) = \dfrac{G}{C} y(k) + \dfrac{B F}{C} u(k)$ | From Diophantine split |
| 4 | Control law | $F u(k) + G y(k) = C y^*(k+d) - \hat{\epsilon}$ | Setpoint-driven MV law |
| 5 | RLS update | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \epsilon(k)$ | Vector update |
| 6 | Kalman gain | $K(k) = \dfrac{P(k-1) \phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T P(k-1) \phi(k)}$ | Dimensionless scalar ratio |
| 7 | Covariance update | $P(k) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[I - K(k)\phi^T(k)\right] P(k-1)$ | Matrix Riccati-like recursion |
| 8 | Forgetting factor constraint | $0.95 \leq \lambda \leq 1.0$ | $\lambda = 1$ → infinite memory |
| 9 | Certainty equivalence substitution | $u(k) = f(\hat{\theta}_{ctrl}(k))$ | $\hat{\theta}_{ctrl}$ replaces true $\theta_{ctrl}$ |
| 10 | Closed-loop pole equation | $A F + q^{-d} B G = C$ | Equivalent to pole placement if $C \to 1$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process control:** Pulp and paper mills, where paper-machine dynamics drift with humidity and pulp composition — direct STR compensates without operator intervention.
- **Autonomous vehicles:** Adaptive cruise control where vehicle mass and road grade change; DSTR avoids the computational burden of solving a Diophantine equation every $T_s$.
- **Hard-disk servo control:** Spindle motor torque constants drift with temperature; DSTR updates the servo compensator directly from track-following error.
- **Robotic manipulators:** Payload changes alter effective inertia; DSTR re-tunes joint controllers in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Direct STR saves computation (no Diophantine solve), but **explicit identification** is required if you want to monitor plant health (e.g., detect a faulty sensor).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Re-parameterization for Direct MV Control

**Step 1: Plant model.** Consider an ARMAX plant of order $n$ with delay $d$:

$$A(q^{-1}) y(k) = B(q^{-1}) u(k-d) + C(q^{-1}) e(k)$$

where
$$A(q^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + a_n q^{-n}$$
$$B(q^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + b_m q^{-m}, \quad b_0 \neq 0$$
$$C(q^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + c_n q^{-n}$$

**Step 2: Decompose via Diophantine equation.** There exist unique polynomials $F(q^{-1})$ of degree $d-1$ and $G(q^{-1})$ of degree $n-1$ satisfying:

$$C(q^{-1}) = A(q^{-1}) F(q^{-1}) + q^{-d} G(q^{-1})$$

$$F(q^{-1}) = 1 + f_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + f_{d-1} q^{-(d-1)}$$
$$G(q^{-1}) = g_0 + g_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + g_{n-1} q^{-(n-1)}$$

**Step 3: Multiply plant equation by $F(q^{-1}) / C(q^{-1}) q^{-d}$.**

$$y(k+d) = \frac{F(q^{-1}) B(q^{-1})}{C(q^{-1})} u(k) + \frac{G(q^{-1})}{C(q^{-1})} y(k) + F(q^{-1}) e(k+d)$$

**Step 4: Take conditional expectation** given $\mathcal{F}_k$ (all data up to time $k$). Since $e(k+d)$ is future white noise with zero mean and $F$ is causal, the optimal $d$-step ahead predictor becomes:

$$\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) = \frac{F B}{C} u(k) + \frac{G}{C} y(k)$$

**Step 5: Minimum-variance control.** Setting $\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) = y^*(k+d)$ gives:

$$\frac{F B}{C} u(k) + \frac{G}{C} y(k) = y^*(k+d)$$

Solving algebraically:

$$B F u(k) = C y^*(k+d) - G y(k)$$

$$u(k) = \frac{C}{B F} y^*(k+d) - \frac{G}{B F} y(k)$$

**Step 6: Expand in regressor form.** Multiply out the predictor equation to get a linear regression. Expand $\dfrac{F B}{C}$ and $\dfrac{G}{C}$ as infinite series (or truncated ARX-like form when $C = 1$). Then the prediction equation is:

$$y(k+d) = \theta^T \phi(k) + \eta(k)$$

where $\theta$ contains *only* $\{g_i\}$ and the coefficients of $FB/C$, and $\phi(k)$ is a vector of past inputs/outputs/setpoints. **This is the re-parameterization** — the regression vector involves no plant $a_i$ or $b_i$ individually.

**Step 7: RLS estimation of $\theta$ directly.**

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\left[y(k) - \hat{\theta}^T(k-1) \phi(k-d)\right]$$

**Step 8: Apply certainty equivalence.** Form the control law:

$$u(k) = \frac{\hat{C}}{\hat{B} \hat{F}} y^*(k+d) - \frac{\hat{G}}{\hat{B} \hat{F}} y(k)$$

with the hat-estimated parameters substituted as if exact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** When $C(q^{-1}) = 1$ (white noise disturbance), the re-parameterization reduces to a *finite-dimensional linear regression* in past $u$ and $y$ — the most common KTU exam setting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recursive Least Squares for DSTR

The DSTR requires online estimation of the controller parameter vector. RLS with forgetting factor is the workhorse:

**Prediction error:**

$$\epsilon(k) = y(k) - \hat{\theta}^T(k-1) \phi(k)$$

**Kalman gain:**

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1) \phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k) P(k-1) \phi(k)}$$

**Parameter update:**

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \epsilon(k)$$

**Covariance update:**

$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[I - K(k) \phi^T(k)\right] P(k-1)$$

**Stability safeguard:** Reset $P(k) = \alpha I$ when $\text{tr}(P(k)) > P_{max}$ to prevent covariance blow-up during persistent excitation loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Convergence Conditions

The DSTR converges to the optimal MV controller provided:

1. **Persistent excitation (PE):** There exists $\delta > 0$ and $N > 0$ such that $\sum_{i=k}^{k+N} \phi(i) \phi^T(i) \geq \delta I$ for all $k$.
2. **Bounded disturbances:** $e(k)$ is a bounded martingale difference sequence.
3. **Minimum-phase plant:** $B(q^{-1})$ has all zeros strictly inside the unit circle (otherwise MV control can yield unbounded $u(k)$).
4. **Known delay:** $d$ is exactly known.

If any of these fail, the closed-loop can become unstable. **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Question on *minimum-phase requirement* is a frequently-tested 3-marker.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Indirect vs Direct STR

| Aspect | Indirect (Explicit) STR | Direct (Implicit) STR |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters estimated | Plant $\{a_i, b_i, c_i\}$ | Controller $\{f_i, g_i\}$ |
| Diophantine solve | Every step | Not required |
| Computational cost | Higher | Lower |
| Implementation complexity | Medium | Low |
| Plant monitoring possible | Yes | No |
| Transient behavior | Slower (two-loop) | Faster (one-loop) |
| Sensitivity to noise model | High | Lower (when $C = 1$) |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Very high |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DSTR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference y star k plus d] --> B[Control Law Formulation]
    Pk[Estimated Controller Parameters theta hat k] --> B
    B --> C[Computed Control Signal u k]
    C --> D[Plant G q inverse]
    D --> E[Process Output y k]
    E --> F[Regressor Builder phi k minus d]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Prediction Error epsilon k]
    E --> G
    G --> H[RLS Estimator]
    H --> Pk
    H --> I[Covariance Matrix P k]
    I --> H
    J[Persistent Excitation Monitor] --> I
    K[Sampling Clock T s] --> B
    K --> F
    K --> H
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Single Sampling Period $k$

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Control Law
    participant PL as Plant
    participant RB as Regressor Builder
    participant RLS as RLS Estimator
    participant MEM as Parameter Memory

    Note over CL: Compute u k using theta hat k-1
    CL->>PL: Apply u k
    PL-->>RB: y k returned
    RB->>RLS: Form phi k minus d
    RLS->>MEM: Update theta hat k and P k
    MEM-->>CL: Provide theta hat k for next k+1
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw I/O Data y k, u k] --> B[Regressor Vector phi]
    B --> C[RLS Engine]
    C --> D[Controller Parameter Estimates]
    D --> E[Certainty Equivalence Controller]
    E --> F[Actuation Signal u k plus 1]
    F --> A
    G[Setpoint r k] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The plant and controller exchange signals via a *single information loop*. The estimator operates on the same data the controller uses — this is what makes the scheme "direct." Compare with indirect STR where the estimator feeds a *separate* plant model that the controller then uses as an intermediate.

## 4.4 Comparison Architecture: Indirect vs Direct STR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Indirect
    I1[Plant I/O] --> I2[Plant Param Estimator]
    I2 --> I3[Diophantine Solver]
    I3 --> I4[Controller]
    I4 --> I1
    end
    subgraph Direct
    J1[Plant I/O] --> J2[Regressor Builder]
    J2 --> J3[Controller Param Estimator]
    J3 --> J4[Controller]
    J4 --> J1
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Direct Self-Tuning Regulator. How does it differ from an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator?**

**Model Answer:**
A Direct Self-Tuning Regulator (DSTR) is an adaptive control scheme that estimates the **controller parameters directly** from observed input–output data, rather than first identifying the plant model. The certainty equivalence principle then substitutes these estimates into the control law.

In an **Indirect (Explicit) STR**, the plant parameters $\{a_i, b_i, c_i\}$ are estimated first, after which a Diophantine equation is solved each step to compute the controller parameters. In a **Direct (Implicit) STR**, the regression vector is chosen such that the same parameters appearing in the minimum-variance or pole-placement control law are directly estimated, eliminating the intermediate design equation.

Key differences:
- **Computational cost:** Direct is lower (no Diophantine solve per step).
- **Plant monitoring:** Indirect provides it; direct does not.
- **Convergence speed:** Direct converges faster because the estimator closes a single loop around the controller, not the plant.

*(3 marks — examiner awards full marks if all three differences plus the defining sentence are present.)*

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain the Certainty Equivalence Principle used in self-tuning regulators.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Certainty Equivalence Principle** states that the unknown true parameters $\theta$ of a plant (or equivalently, the true optimal controller parameters $\theta^*$) appearing in the optimal control law may be **replaced by their current estimates $\hat{\theta}(k)$**, computed at every sampling instant. The control law is then implemented as if $\hat{\theta}(k)$ were the true parameter vector.

**Why it works:** Under persistent excitation and minimum-phase assumptions, the parameter estimate converges to the true value; before convergence, certainty equivalence yields a *sub-optimal but stabilizing* controller that progressively improves. **Aström and Wittenmark (1973, 1989)** formalized this in the original minimum-variance self-tuner.

*(3 marks — 1 for statement, 2 for explanation. Examiner awards 0.5 if "principle" is omitted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B (Module Internal Choice)  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the minimum-variance control law for a plant described by
$$A(q^{-1}) y(k) = B(q^{-1}) u(k-1) + C(q^{-1}) e(k)$$
with $A = 1 + a_1 q^{-1}$, $B = b_0$, $C = 1$, $d = 1$. Show clearly how the regression vector for direct estimation is constructed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the RLS algorithm used in the direct self-tuning regulator and explain the role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$. Under what conditions will the parameter estimates diverge? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Since $d = 1$, the Diophantine equation $C = A F + q^{-1} G$ gives:
- $F = 1$ (degree 0, since $d-1 = 0$)
- $G = (C - A F) q = (1 - 1 - a_1 q^{-1}) q = -a_1$ — wait, redo correctly.

Actually: $C(q^{-1}) = 1$, $A(q^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1}$, $F(q^{-1}) = 1$.

$$1 = (1 + a_1 q^{-1})(1) + q^{-1} G(q^{-1})$$
$$G(q^{-1}) = -a_1$$

So the one-step ahead predictor is:

$$\hat{y}(k+1 \mid k) = -a_1 y(k) + b_0 u(k)$$

Setting $\hat{y}(k+1) = y^*(k+1)$:

$$u(k) = \frac{1}{b_0}\left[y^*(k+1) + a_1 y(k)\right]$$

The regression vector is:

$$\phi(k) = \begin{bmatrix} y(k) \\ u(k) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \theta = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**However, this $\theta$ contains plant parameters, not controller parameters directly.** To make it *direct*, we re-write the control law itself as the regression:

$$y(k+1) = y^*(k+1) + \epsilon(k+1)$$

Substituting the control law:

$$y(k+1) = y^*(k+1) \Rightarrow u(k) = \frac{y^*(k+1) - \hat{y}(k+1 \mid k) + \hat{y}(k+1 \mid k)}{b_0}$$

The direct form regressor for $\theta = [b_0^{-1}, a_1 b_0^{-1}]^T$ with $\phi = [y^*(k+1), y(k)]^T$ gives:

$$y(k+1) = \theta^T \phi(k) + \text{noise}$$

So the controller parameters $b_0^{-1}$ and $a_1 b_0^{-1}$ are *directly* estimated.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for Diophantine, 2 for predictor, 2 for control law, 1 for regressor construction.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

RLS with forgetting factor $\lambda$:

1. Initialize: $\hat{\theta}(0) = 0$, $P(0) = \alpha I$, $\alpha = 1000$.
2. At each step $k$:
   - Read regressor $\phi(k)$ and output $y(k)$.
   - Innovation: $\epsilon(k) = y(k) - \hat{\theta}^T(k-1) \phi(k)$.
   - Kalman gain: $K(k) = \dfrac{P(k-1) \phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k) P(k-1) \phi(k)}$.
   - Update: $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \epsilon(k)$.
   - Covariance: $P(k) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}[I - K(k) \phi^T(k)] P(k-1)$.
3. Control update: $u(k) = \hat{\theta}_1(k) y^*(k+1) + \hat{\theta}_2(k) y(k)$.

**Role of $\lambda$:** The forgetting factor exponentially down-weights past data with weight $\lambda^{k-i}$. For $\lambda < 1$, old measurements contribute less, allowing the estimator to track **time-varying parameters**. Typical value $\lambda = 0.98$. When $\lambda = 1$, the algorithm has *infinite memory* and cannot adapt to drift.

**Divergence conditions:** Parameter estimates diverge when:
1. Loss of persistent excitation: $\phi(k)$ stays in a lower-dimensional subspace for too long, causing $P(k)$ to grow unbounded.
2. Non-minimum-phase plant: $B(q^{-1})$ has roots outside the unit circle, generating unbounded $u(k)$.
3. Open-loop unstable plant with incorrect delay estimate: $d$ wrong by even one step.
4. Unmodelled dynamics combined with high adaptive gain.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for RLS steps, 2 for forgetting factor, 3 for divergence conditions — examiner deducts 1 mark per missing condition.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Direct Self-Tuning Regulator with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the regressor builder in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the plant $y(k) - 0.9 y(k-1) = 0.5 u(k-1) + e(k)$, design the direct minimum-variance self-tuning regulator. Identify the regression vector and the controller parameters to be estimated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Direct STR architecture has four main blocks:

1. **Plant:** Generates $y(k)$ from $u(k-d)$ and disturbance.
2. **Regressor Builder:** Constructs the regression vector $\phi(k-d)$ from past inputs, outputs, and setpoints — chosen such that the unknown parameters are exactly the controller coefficients.
3. **RLS Estimator:** Updates $\hat{\theta}(k)$ and $P(k)$ using the prediction error $\epsilon(k)$.
4. **Control Law:** Implements $u(k) = -\hat{\theta}^T(k) \phi(k) + \text{setpoint correction}$ via certainty equivalence.

**Role of the Regressor Builder:**
- Converts raw I/O into the *information vector* used by the estimator.
- The form of $\phi(k)$ determines whether estimation is **direct** or **indirect**. By choosing $\phi$ to contain $[y(k), u(k-1), y(k-1), \ldots]$ with coefficients matching the MV law, the estimator output is *already* the controller.
- Implementation in practice: shift registers with delays $q^{-1}, q^{-2}, \ldots, q^{-n}$ and optional filtering $1/C(q^{-1})$ when $C \neq 1$.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for block diagram, 2 for regressor builder role, 2 for direct vs indirect distinction.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**
