Plant: $y(k) = 0.9 y(k-1) + 0.5 u(k-1) + e(k)$, i.e., $a_1 = -0.9$, $b_0 = 0.5$, $C = 1$, $d = 1$.

Minimum-variance predictor (one-step):

$$\hat{y}(k+1 \mid k) = 0.9 y(k) + 0.5 u(k)$$

Control law (setpoint $r$):

$$u(k) = \frac{r(k+1) - 0.9 y(k)}{0.5} = 2 r(k+1) - 1.8 y(k)$$

To make this **direct**, the control law is rewritten as:

$$y(k+1) = \theta^T \phi(k) + e(k+1)$$
with
$$\theta = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \phi(k) = \begin{bmatrix} r(k+1) \\ y(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

The RLS estimates $\hat{\theta}_1(k) \to 2$ and $\hat{\theta}_2(k) \to -1.8$. The control at each step becomes:

$$u(k) = \hat{\theta}_1(k) r(k+1) + \hat{\theta}_2(k) y(k)$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for predictor, 2 for control law, 2 for regression form, 1 for numerical accuracy.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DSTR Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up direct and indirect formulations** — students often write the *plant parameter regressor* $\phi = [y(k-1), u(k-1)]^T$ instead of the *controller parameter regressor* $\phi = [r(k+d), y(k)]^T$. Examiner deducts **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the persistence of excitation condition** — many answers state convergence without listing PE, leading to a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda$ with the eigenvalue of the closed loop** — $\lambda$ is the *forgetting factor* in $(0,1)$, not the system pole. Many students lose a mark by writing $\lambda < 0$.
> 4. **Omitting the certainty equivalence statement** — even when the calculation is correct, the absence of the *principle* name yields partial credit only.
> 5. **Diophantine degree errors** — $F$ has degree $d-1$, not $d$. Students frequently write $F = 1 + f_1 q^{-1}$ when $d = 1$. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Numerical slip in Diophantine coefficients** — sign errors are common; double-check $(C - AF) q^{d}$ carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DSTR** estimates *controller* parameters directly; **ISTR** estimates *plant* parameters first.
- The defining algebraic step is **re-parameterization** so the regression vector contains controller coefficients.
- **Certainty Equivalence** substitutes $\hat{\theta}(k)$ for $\theta^*$ in the control law.
- **RLS with forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0.95, 1.0)$** is the standard online estimator; covariance $P(k)$ must be reset on divergence.
- **Minimum-phase** requirement: $B(q^{-1})$ must have all zeros strictly inside the unit circle.
- **Persistent excitation** is necessary for parameter convergence; loss of PE causes $P(k)$ blow-up.
- **Delay $d$ must be known exactly** — a one-step error destabilizes the closed loop.
- The direct method has **lower computational cost** (no Diophantine solve per step) but loses the ability to monitor plant parameters for diagnostics.
- The minimum-variance self-tuner is a special case of DSTR where the design equation reduces to the regression itself (when $C = 1$).
- For KTU 2024 exams, **always** draw the block diagram and explicitly state which parameters are being estimated — both are expected in any 14-mark answer.
- **Aström–Wittenmark milestone (1973):** the original self-tuning minimum-variance regulator; subsequent work by Clarke, Gawthrop, Narendra, and Goodwin extended it to generalized minimum-variance, pole-placement, and model-reference adaptive variants.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to "design a DSTR," always present your answer in four parts — (i) Plant model & assumptions, (ii) Diophantine solution & predictor, (iii) Regressor & RLS, (iv) Control law & certainty equivalence. This structure guarantees full marks with the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disturbances with Known Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Disturbances with Known Characteristics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In adaptive and robust control theory, a **disturbance with known characteristics** refers to an external perturbation $d(t)$ acting on a control system whose **mathematical structure** (i.e., the family of functions to which it belongs) is **completely known a priori**, even though its instantaneous amplitude, phase, or arrival time may be unknown. Formally, such a disturbance is generated by a **known linear autonomous exosystem** (also called the disturbance generator) of the form: $$\dot{w}_d(t) = A_d \, w_d(t), \qquad d(t) = C_d \, w_d(t)$$ where $A_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n_d \times n_d}$ is a known constant matrix whose eigenvalues lie on the **imaginary axis** (marginal stability) and $w_d(0)$ is the unknown initial condition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The eigenvalues of $A_d$ being on the $j\omega$-axis (not in the open right-half plane) is what qualifies the disturbance as *bounded* and *persistent*. This is the foundation of the **Internal Model Principle (IMP)** by Francis and Wonham (1976). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cruise Control Going Uphill" Imagine driving a car with **cruise control** on a road whose slope changes. The **gravity force** pulling the car backward is a *disturbance*. If the road's gradient profile is **known** (say, a hilly terrain with sinusoidal undulation), the cruise controller can *anticipate* and *pre-compensate* the throttle before the slope actually changes. - The **shape of the disturbance** = the road's profile (sinusoidal hill). - The **amplitude / phase** = unknown (different hill heights). - The **control action** = feedforward throttle boost to neutralize gravity. This is precisely the engineering meaning of "disturbance with known characteristics" — the **pattern** is known, the **magnitude** is estimated. ## 1.3 Classification of Disturbances with Known Characteristics | **Class** | **Time Function** | **Exosystem Eigenvalues** | **Physical Example** | |---|---|---|---| | **Constant (Step)** | $d(t) = d_0$ | $s = 0$ | Steady wind pressure on antenna | | **Ramp** | $d(t) = d_0 + d_1 t$ | $s^2 = 0$ | Slowly drifting sensor bias | | **Polynomial of degree $n$** | $d(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i t^i$ | $s^{n+1} = 0$ | Gravity-induced load on elevator | | **Sinusoidal** | $d(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | $s^2 + \omega^2 = 0$ | Power-line 50 Hz interference | | **Multi-sinusoidal** | $d(t) = \sum A_i \sin(\omega_i t + \phi_i)$ | $\prod (s^2 + \omega_i^2) = 0$ | Harmonic vibration in machines | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** The order of the exosystem $n_d$ directly dictates the number of **integrators** (or resonant modes) that the controller must internally contain to achieve **perfect asymptotic rejection**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Internal Model Principle (IMP) — The Central Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statement (Francis & Wonham, 1976):** *A feedback control system achieves perfect asymptotic disturbance rejection (or perfect reference tracking) of a signal generated by an exosystem with eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ if and only if the loop transfer function $L(s)$ contains a model of the exosystem — i.e., the characteristic polynomial of $L(s)$ includes the factors $(s - \lambda_i)$ for every unstable or marginally-stable mode of the disturbance.* In transfer-function form, the controller $C(s)$ must contain the **denominator** of the disturbance model: $$C(s) = \frac{N_c(s)}{D_c(s)}, \quad \text{where} \quad D_c(s) \supseteq \text{char-poly}(A_d)$$ ## 2.2 Augmented Plant Representation When the disturbance is generated by an exosystem, the **augmented state vector** combines the plant and the disturbance generator: $$x_a(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ w_d(t) \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \dot{x}_a = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} A & B_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix}}_{A_a} x_a + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} B \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{B_a} u(t)$$ $$y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} C & C_d \end{bmatrix} x_a(t)$$ where: - $A, B, C$ are the plant matrices of the nominal system. - $B_d$ is the disturbance input matrix (channel through which $d$ enters the plant). - $A_d$ is the known disturbance dynamics matrix. ## 2.3 Disturbance Observer / Estimator Design A **Luenberger-style observer** is constructed for the augmented system: $$\dot{\hat{x}}_a = A_a \hat{x}_a + B_a u + L_a (y - C_a \hat{x}_a)$$ The observer gain $L_a = \begin{bmatrix} L_x \\ L_d \end{bmatrix}$ is chosen so that $(A_a - L_a C_a)$ is **Hurwitz**, giving exponentially converging estimates $\hat{x}(t) \to x(t)$ and $\hat{w}_d(t) \to w_d(t)$. ## 2.4 Control Law with Feedforward Compensation Once the disturbance state $w_d$ is estimated, the control law becomes: $$u(t) = -K_x \hat{x}(t) - K_d \hat{w}_d(t) + r(t)$$ The term $-K_d \hat{w}_d$ is the **feedforward cancellation** — it directly cancels the disturbance's effect before it propagates through the plant. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are listed in the KTU-recommended symbol conventions. | **Formula / Identity** | **Description** | **Typical Use** | |---|---|---| | $\dot{w}_d = A_d w_d$ | Exosystem equation | Defines disturbance dynamics | | $d(t) = C_d w_d(t)$ | Disturbance output equation | Maps exosystem state to physical $d$ | | $A_a = \begin{bmatrix} A & B_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix}$ | Augmented open-loop matrix | Builds observer/controller | | $L_a = (A_a - L_a C_a)$ Hurwitz | Observer stability condition | Design of $\hat{x}, \hat{w}_d$ | | $A_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix} A - BK_x & B_d - BK_d \\ -L_d C & A_d - L_d C_d \end{bmatrix}$ | Closed-loop augmented matrix | Stability proof | | $u = -K_x \hat{x} - K_d \hat{w}_d + r$ | Combined feedback + feedforward law | Disturbance cancellation | | $\text{char-poly}(A_d) \mid \text{char-poly}(L_{loop})$ | **IMP condition** | Perfect rejection test | | $\rho(A_d) \subset j\omega\text{-axis}$ | Boundedness requirement | Disturbance admissibility | | $n_d = \dim(w_d)$ | Exosystem order | Number of integrators / resonators needed | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The "disturbance with known characteristics" framework is foundational in: - **Hard disk drive servo control** — rejecting 50/60 Hz power-line vibration. - **Aircraft autopilot** — countering known periodic gust spectra (von Kármán model). - **Wind turbine control** — rejecting 1P and 3P rotational harmonics. - **Active suspension in vehicles** — canceling road-induced vibrations at known wheel frequencies. - **Power electronics** — eliminating harmonics at $\omega = n\omega_0$ (n=3, 5, 7…). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Augmented Plant Model (from First Principles) We start with the nominal plant: $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) + B_d d(t)$$ $$y(t) = C x(t)$$ The disturbance $d(t)$ is generated by a known exosystem: $$\dot{w}_d(t) = A_d w_d(t), \quad d(t) = C_d w_d(t)$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $d(t) = C_d w_d(t)$ into the plant equation: $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) + B_d C_d \, w_d(t)$$ **Step 2:** Write the disturbance state equation in expanded form: $$\dot{w}_d(t) = 0 \cdot x(t) + A_d w_d(t) + 0 \cdot u(t)$$ **Step 3:** Stack the two equations vertically to form $\dot{x}_a = A_a x_a + B_a u$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ w_d(t) \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} A & B_d C_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ w_d(t) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u(t) \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on notation:** Some textbooks absorb $C_d$ into $B_d$ (defining $\tilde{B}_d = B_d C_d$). KTU convention treats $B_d$ as the *effective* disturbance input matrix for brevity, giving the cleaner $A_a$ form shown in §2.2. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Stability under Observer-Based Control **Setup:** Plant + exosystem under the control law $u = -K_x \hat{x} - K_d \hat{w}_d$ (zero reference, $r=0$). **Step 1 — True system dynamics:** Substitute $u$ into the plant: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= A x + B(-K_x \hat{x} - K_d \hat{w}_d) + B_d C_d w_d \\ &= (A - B K_x) x + B K_x (x - \hat{x}) - B K_d (w_d - \hat{w}_d) + B_d C_d w_d \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Observer error dynamics:** Define $e_x = x - \hat{x}$, $e_d = w_d - \hat{w}_d$. Then: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{e}_x &= (A - L_x C) e_x + (B_d C_d - L_x C_d) e_d \\ \dot{e}_d &= -L_d C \, e_x + (A_d - L_d C_d) e_d \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Combined closed-loop matrix** in coordinates $(x, e_x, e_d)$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ e_x \\ e_d \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} A - B K_x & B K_x & -B K_d \\ 0 & A - L_x C & B_d C_d - L_x C_d \\ 0 & -L_d C & A_d - L_d C_d \end{bmatrix}}_{A_{cl}} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ e_x \\ e_d \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Stability criterion:** Because $A_{cl}$ is **block triangular**, its eigenvalues are the union of: $$\sigma(A_{cl}) = \sigma(A - B K_x) \cup \sigma\!\begin{bmatrix} A - L_x C & B_d C_d - L_x C_d \\ -L_d C & A_d - L_d C_d \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The system is asymptotically stable **if and only if** $(A, B)$ is stabilizable (feedback design) and $(A_a, C_a)$ is detectable (observer design). This **separates the two design problems** — a cornerstone of adaptive control. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Internal Model Principle for Sinusoidal Disturbance Consider a single-tone sinusoidal disturbance $d(t) = A_0 \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi_0)$ with known frequency $\omega_0$ and unknown amplitude/phase. **Step 1 — Exosystem construction:** The minimal realization is: $$A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \omega_0 \\ -\omega_0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ since the characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A_d) = s^2 + \omega_0^2 = 0$ generates $\sin$ and $\cos$. **Step 2 — IMP requirement:** The controller's denominator must contain the factor $(s^2 + \omega_0^2)$: $$C(s) = \frac{N_c(s)}{(s^2 + \omega_0^2) \cdot D'(s)}$$ **Step 3 — Loop transfer function with plant $G_p(s)$:** The sensitivity function becomes: $$S(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G_p(s) C(s)}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at the disturbance frequency:** Since the denominator of $C(s)$ vanishes at $s = \pm j\omega_0$, the loop gain $L(s) = G_p(s) C(s) \to \infty$ at $s = j\omega_0$, hence: $$S(j\omega_0) = \frac{1}{1 + L(j\omega_0)} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Output response:** The forced output to the disturbance is: $$y_{ss}(t) = \vert G_p(j\omega_0) \vert \cdot \vert S(j\omega_0) \vert \cdot A_0 = 0$$ $$\boxed{\,y_{ss}(t) \to 0 \quad \text{(perfect asymptotic rejection)}\,}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Disturbance Accommodation via State Augmentation (DAC) The **Disturbance Accommodation Control (DAC)** framework (by C.D. Johnson, 1971) gives an explicit controller design. **Step 1 — Augmented state dynamics (plant + exosystem):** $$\dot{x}_a = A_a x_a + B_a u$$ **Step 2 — Solve the algebraic Riccati equation (ARE)** for the augmented system: $$A_a^T P + P A_a - P B_a R^{-1} B_a^T P + Q = 0$$ where $Q \geq 0$, $R > 0$ are design weights. **Step 3 — Optimal control law:** $$u^*(t) = -R^{-1} B_a^T P \, \hat{x}_a(t) = -K_a \hat{x}_a(t)$$ **Step 4 — Disturbance accommodation term:** Expand $K_a = [K_x \;\; K_d]$: $$\begin{aligned} u^*(t) &= -K_x \hat{x}(t) - K_d \hat{w}_d(t) \\ \underbrace{u^*}_{\text{total}} &= \underbrace{-K_x \hat{x}}_{\text{state feedback}} \; + \; \underbrace{(-K_d \hat{w}_d)}_{\text{disturbance accommodation}} \end{aligned}$$ The second term is the **feedforward compensation** that *accommodates* the disturbance rather than merely rejecting its effect after the fact. > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** Feedback alone ($-K_x \hat{x}$) is reactive — it acts *after* the disturbance has already perturbed the state. The feedforward term ($-K_d \hat{w}_d$) is *proactive* — it cancels the disturbance at its source. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Adaptive Control with Known-Disturbance Rejection ```mermaid flowchart LR Ref["Reference r(t)"] --> SummA(("+")) SummA --> Plant["Plant Gp(s)"] Plant --> Output["Output y(t)"] Output --> SummB(("+")) DistExo["Exosystem A_d, C_d"] --> DistSig["Disturbance d(t)"] DistSig --> SummB SummB --> MeasY["Measured y(t)"] MeasY --> Observer["Augmented Observer
L_a design"] Plant -.->|"x(t)"| Observer DistExo -.->|"w_d(t)"| Observer Observer --> StateEst["State Estimate x̂(t)"] Observer --> DistEst["Disturbance Estimate ŵ_d(t)"] SummA --> ErrSig["Error e(t)"] ErrSig --> GainKx["Gain K_x"] DistEst --> GainKd["Gain K_d"] Ref --> GainKr["Gain K_r"] GainKx --> SummC(("+")) GainKd --> SummC GainKr --> SummC SummC --> ControlU["Control u(t)"] ControlU --> Plant ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **green/blue path** (top) is the standard feedback loop. - The **disturbance channel** (middle) shows $d(t)$ entering the plant from the exosystem $A_d$. - The **observer** receives both the measured output and the known $A_d$ model, producing estimates $\hat{x}$ and $\hat{w}_d$. - The control law combines **three signals**: $-K_x \hat{x}$ (stabilizing feedback), $-K_d \hat{w}_d$ (disturbance feedforward), and $K_r r$ (reference scaling). ## 4.2 The Internal Model Principle — Structural View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PlantSide["PLANT SIDE"] P1["G_p(s) - Nominal Plant"] D1["Exosystem A_d
(disturbance generator)"] end subgraph ControllerSide["CONTROLLER SIDE (must contain copy of A_d)"] C1["C(s) = N_c(s) / D_c(s)"] IM1["Internal Model
1 / char-poly(A_d)"] C1 --- IM1 end P1 -->|"y(t)"| C1 D1 -->|"d(t)"| P1 C1 -->|"u(t)"| P1 style IM1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key visual:** The shaded block `IM1` labeled *"Internal Model"* sits *inside* the controller. This duplication of the disturbance dynamics is the **structural essence** of the IMP — without it, $S(j\omega_0) \neq 0$ and rejection is imperfect. ## 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram — Observer-Coupled Control ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Estimator["STATE & DISTURBANCE ESTIMATOR"] O1["x̂_dot = A x̂ + B u + B_d C_d ŵ_d + L_x (y - C x̂)"] O2["ŵ_d_dot = A_d ŵ_d + L_d (y - C x̂)"] end O1 --> O3["x̂(t)"] O2 --> O4["ŵ_d(t)"] O3 --> KxMul["× (-K_x)"] O4 --> KdMul["× (-K_d)"] KxMul --> SumU(("Σ")) KdMul --> SumU SumU --> Uout["u(t)"] Uout --> O1 Ysig["y(t)"] --> O1 Ysig --> O2 ``` This diagram highlights the **separation principle** — observer dynamics and controller dynamics are designed *independently* and then coupled through the estimate $\hat{x}_a$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** ### **Q1.** *Define a "disturbance with known characteristics." Give two examples of such disturbances along with their exosystem matrices $A_d$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A disturbance with known characteristics is an external perturbation whose **mathematical structure** (i.e., the dynamical system that generates it) is fully known, although its instantaneous amplitude and/or phase are unknown. It is generated by a **known linear autonomous exosystem** $\dot{w}_d = A_d w_d$, $d = C_d w_d$, where the eigenvalues of $A_d$ lie on the $j\omega$-axis. **Example 1 — Constant (step) disturbance:** $d(t) = d_0$. Exosystem: $A_d = [0]$, $C_d = [1]$, initial condition $w_d(0) = d_0$ unknown. **Example 2 — Sinusoidal disturbance:** $d(t) = A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$. Exosystem: $$A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \omega_0 \\ -\omega_0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Eigenvalues $\pm j\omega_0$ are on the imaginary axis; amplitude $A$ and phase $\phi$ are encoded in the unknown initial condition $w_d(0)$. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** ### **Q2.** *State the Internal Model Principle (IMP) and explain its physical significance in disturbance rejection.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (Francis & Wonham, 1976):** A linear feedback control system achieves **perfect asymptotic rejection** of a disturbance generated by an exosystem with characteristic polynomial $\Delta_d(s)$ **if and only if** the characteristic polynomial of the **open-loop transfer function** $L(s) = G_p(s) C(s)$ contains $\Delta_d(s)$ as a factor. **Physical significance:** - (1) The controller must *internally replicate* a model of the disturbance generator — like an "antibody" that recognizes the disturbance's pattern. - (2) This makes the sensitivity function $S(j\omega) = 0$ at the disturbance frequency, ensuring zero steady-state error. - (3) It applies **symmetrically** to reference tracking — the controller must also contain a model of the reference signal generator. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly claim the IMP applies only to *constant* disturbances (e.g., step inputs). The principle is **general** — it applies to *any* exosystem with eigenvalues on the $j\omega$-axis (ramps, sinusoids, multi-tone). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Question Choice Set]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Consider the SISO plant $$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} d, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x$$ subject to a **sinusoidal disturbance** $d(t) = 0.5 \sin(2t)$ of known frequency $\omega_0 = 2$ rad/s but unknown amplitude/phase. Augment the plant with the disturbance exosystem and write the complete augmented system $(A_a, B_a, C_a)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a state-feedback gain $K_x$ and a feedforward gain $K_d$ such that the closed-loop system achieves **zero steady-state error** to the disturbance. Justify your design by invoking the Internal Model Principle. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Augmentation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Identify the plant matrices: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Construct the sinusoidal exosystem with $\omega_0 = 2$: $$A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix]$$ **Step 3:** Form the augmented matrices (using $B_d$ as the effective input to the plant, $n = 2$ plant states, $n_d = 2$ disturbance states): $$\begin{aligned} A_a &= \begin{bmatrix} A & B_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ -2 & -3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_a = \begin{bmatrix} B \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\ C_a &= \begin{bmatrix} C & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ > **[7 marks awarded — full marks for correctly identifying $A_d$, $B_d$, and assembling $A_a$, $B_a$, $C_a$.]** **Part (b) — Controller Design with IMP Justification (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Invoke IMP:** For perfect rejection of a sinusoidal disturbance of frequency $\omega_0 = 2$, the **characteristic polynomial of the open-loop transfer function** must contain $(s^2 + 4)$. Equivalently, the controller must contain poles at $s = \pm j2$. **Step 2 — Design the feedback gain $K_x$:** Use **pole placement** for the pair $(A, B)$ to place the two plant closed-loop poles (say) at $s = -2$ and $s = -3$: $$K_x = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ The desired characteristic polynomial: $\alpha_d(s) = s^2 + 5s + 6$. Using Ackermann's formula or direct comparison: $$K_x = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ (Verification: $\det(sI - A + BK_x) = s^2 + 5s + 6$ ✓) **Step 3 — Design the feedforward gain $K_d$:** The IMP requires that the augmented controller gain $K_a = [K_x \;\; K_d]$ places the **exosystem eigenvalues $\pm j2$** as eigenvalues of $A_a - B_a K_a$. This means the augmented plant has the additional **resonant mode** at $\pm j2$ in the loop, which is exactly the IMP condition. A systematic way: solve the **regulator equation** $$\begin{aligned} (A_a - B_a K_a)^T P_a + P_a (A_a - B_a K_a) &= -I_{4 \times 4} \end{aligned}$$ to get a stabilizing $K_a$, then read off $K_d$. The resulting structure of $C(s)$ contains the factor $(s^2 + 4)$, confirming IMP is satisfied. **Step 4 — Physical justification:** The controller $C(s)$ now has the form $$C(s) = \frac{N_c(s)}{(s^2 + 4) D'(s)}$$ The denominator zero at $s = \pm j2$ makes the loop gain $L(j2) = G_p(j2) C(j2) \to \infty$, forcing $S(j2) = 0$ and hence $y_{ss}(t) \to 0$. The disturbance is **completely rejected** at steady state. > **[7 marks awarded — 2 marks for invoking IMP, 3 marks for $K_x$ design, 2 marks for $K_d$ reasoning and physical justification.]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain the **Disturbance Accommodation Control (DAC)** approach of C.D. Johnson. Derive the optimal control law and clearly identify the disturbance-accommodation term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A second-order plant $$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 1}$$ is subject to a **ramp disturbance** $d(t) = 0.1 t$. Construct the exosystem, augment the plant, and verify whether a **PI controller** suffices for perfect rejection, or whether a higher-order controller is required. Justify using the IMP. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — DAC Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Plant + Exosystem:** $$\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + B_d d, \quad \dot{w}_d = A_d w_d, \quad d = C_d w_d$$ **Step 2 — Form augmented dynamics:** $$\dot{x}_a = A_a x_a + B_a u$$ **Step 3 — Form the Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE):** $$A_a^T P + P A_a - P B_a R^{-1} B_a^T P + Q = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve and obtain the optimal gain:** $$K_a = R^{-1} B_a^T P = \begin{bmatrix} K_x & K_d \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Optimal control law:** $$\begin{aligned} u^*(t) &= -K_x \hat{x}(t) - K_d \hat{w}_d(t) + r(t) \\ \underbrace{u^*}_{\text{total control}} &= \underbrace{-K_x \hat{x}}_{\text{state feedback}} \; + \; \underbrace{(-K_d \hat{w}_d)}_{\textbf{disturbance accommodation}} \; + \; r \end{aligned}$$ The third term ($-K_d \hat{w}_d$) **accommodates** the disturbance — it is *proactive* compensation, not reactive. > **[7 marks — 2 for the ARE setup, 3 for deriving $u^*$, 2 for identifying the accommodation term.]** **Part (b) — Ramp Disturbance & PI Limitation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Exosystem for ramp:** $d(t) = 0.1 t$ is generated by: $$A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad w_d(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Eigenvalues: $0, 0$ (double pole at origin). **Step 2 — Required IMP structure:** For perfect rejection of a **ramp**, the controller must contain $(s^2) = 0$ in its denominator — i.e., **two integrators**. **Step 3 — PI controller analysis:** A PI controller is $$C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$$ The denominator has **only one** $s$-factor — i.e., **one integrator**. This is sufficient to reject a **step** disturbance (one $s$ needed) but **insufficient** to reject a **ramp** (two $s$'s needed). **Step 4 — Conclusion:** A PI controller will result in a **non-zero steady-state error** for a ramp disturbance. The minimum adequate controller is a **double-integrator** structure (often called a **PI²** or **PID with two integrators**): $$C(s) = \frac{K_p s^2 + K_i s + K_d}{s^2}$$ **Step 5 — IMP verification:** The open-loop characteristic polynomial $\text{char}(L(s)) = \text{char}(G_p(s)) \cdot \text{char}(C(s))$ now contains $s^2 \cdot (s^2 + 2s + 1) = s^2(s+1)^2$, which **includes the exosystem factor $s^2$**. Therefore, the IMP is satisfied and $y_{ss}(t) \to 0$ for the ramp disturbance. ✓ > **[7 marks — 2 for ramp exosystem, 2 for showing PI has one integrator, 2 for IMP-based conclusion, 1 for the recommended controller form.]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):** > 1. **Confusing $B_d$ and $B_d C_d$** — In augmentation, write $\begin{bmatrix} A & B_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix}$ if $B_d$ already absorbs $C_d$, or $\begin{bmatrix} A & B_d C_d \\ 0 & A_d \end{bmatrix}$ otherwise. **Be consistent with your notation.** > 2. **Forgetting the feedforward term** — When asked to "achieve zero steady-state error to a disturbance," students often design only $-K_x \hat{x}$ and forget $-K_d \hat{w}_d$. **Both are required** for perfect rejection. > 3. **Counting integrators incorrectly** — A ramp needs **two** integrators, a parabola needs **three**, etc. The order $n_d$ of the exosystem equals the number of integrators (or paired resonators for sinusoids). > 4. **IMP does not apply to unstable disturbances** — If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ for some $\lambda_i \in \sigma(A_d)$, no LTI controller can reject it asymptotically. **Boundedness is required.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-Second Revision Pass** - ✅ A **disturbance with known characteristics** is generated by a known exosystem $\dot{w}_d = A_d w_d$, $d = C_d w_d$ with $\sigma(A_d) \subset j\omega$-axis. - ✅ The **Internal Model Principle (IMP)** states: *perfect rejection* requires the controller to *internally contain a copy* of the exosystem dynamics. - ✅ **Number of integrators** in the controller = dimension of the polynomial part of the exosystem = $n_d$ for an $n_d$-th degree polynomial disturbance. - ✅ **Sinusoidal disturbances** require *paired* imaginary-axis poles $(s^2 + \omega_0^2)$ in the controller. - ✅ The **augmented plant** is built by stacking plant and exosystem: $A_a = \text{blkdiag}(A, A_d)$ with the $(1,2)$ block $= B_d$. - ✅ **Separation Principle** holds: design $K_x$ for $(A, B)$ and $L_a$ for $(A_a, C_a)$ *independently*. - ✅ **Observer-based control law:** $u = -K_x \hat{x} - K_d \hat{w}_d + r$. The $-K_d \hat{w}_d$ term is the **feedforward cancellation**. - ✅ **Steady-state rejection:** $y_{ss} = G_p(j\omega) S(j\omega) D(j\omega) \to 0$ when $S(j\omega) = 0$, which is guaranteed by IMP. - ✅ **Disturbance Accommodation Control (DAC)** uses LQR on the augmented system to *optimally* trade off regulation and accommodation. - ✅ **Common KTU question types:** (1) construct augmented model, (2) design $K_x$ via pole placement, (3) determine required controller order via IMP, (4) verify $y_{ss} = 0$ for a given disturbance class. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Disturbances with Known Characteristics** 🎯

topics.title: Design of Minimum Variance and Moving Average Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design of Minimum Variance and Moving Average Controllers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Minimum Variance (MV) Controller **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The **Minimum Variance Controller** is a one-step-ahead optimal stochastic controller introduced by **Åström (1970)** that minimizes the variance of the output (or the mean square error) of a system subject to random disturbances. For a single-input single-output (SISO) ARMAX plant, the MV controller generates a control signal that drives the future output as close to the set-point as possible, sacrificing minimum mean-square output error. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The MV controller is the *first historical* self-tuning regulator. It forms the theoretical foundation of all explicit and implicit adaptive control schemes covered in this module. #### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Weather-Savvy Helmsman Imagine steering a small boat in turbulent, gusty waters. You cannot eliminate the wind gusts, but you can adjust the rudder **just early enough** so that the boat's next position lands as close as possible to the desired track. The helmsman estimates where the boat *will* be in the next instant (one step ahead) and steers to cancel that predicted deviation. This is precisely what the **Minimum Variance Controller** does — it *anticipates* the next output using a predictor and commands a control action to make the predicted deviation **zero**. --- ### 1.2 Moving Average (MA) Controller **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The **Moving Average (MA) Controller** is a generalization of the minimum-variance controller that minimizes a weighted sum of future output variances over a finite control horizon $\mathbf{N}$ (often $\mathbf{N \ge 1}$). Unlike the pure MV controller (where the control acts only at step $k$), the MA controller allows the control signal to remain constant over a pre-specified *minimum move horizon* $N_u$, producing a sequence of "moving-average" weighted control actions that act on the process over multiple future instants. > [!NOTE] > **Why MA?** Pure MV control can be aggressive, producing large control moves and even destabilizing non-minimum-phase plants. The MA controller extends the prediction/control horizon, yielding smoother, more robust control action at the cost of slightly higher output variance. #### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Cruise-Control with Look-Ahead Radar Consider a modern adaptive cruise control that doesn't only react to the *immediate* distance gap, but uses a radar to scan traffic over the next $N$ seconds and plans a smooth braking/acceleration profile. It tolerates a small speed deviation now to avoid harsh braking later. This is the essence of the **MA controller** — it trades a tiny bit of *immediate* output variance for *smoother, more practical* control over an extended horizon. --- ### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features | Property | Minimum Variance Controller | Moving Average Controller | |---|---|---| | Prediction horizon | $k$ (one-step-ahead) | $N$ (multi-step) | | Control horizon | Single move at instant $t$ | Constant $u$ over $N_u$ steps | | Control variance | Minimum possible | Slightly higher than MV | | Control effort | Often high | Reduced / smoother | | Robustness to NMP | Poor (instability risk) | Better (tunable) | | Application | Benchmark / reference | Practical industrial adaptive control | > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) answers, always begin by **identifying the plant model form (ARMAX)**, then state whether the question demands a **single-step** (MV) or **multi-step** (MA) regulator. This immediately shows the examiner that you have understood the design intent. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Controlled ARMAX Plant Model Consider the discrete-time SISO stochastic plant widely used in adaptive control theory: $$A(z^{-1}) y(t) = z^{-k} B(z^{-1}) u(t) + C(z^{-1}) e(t)$$ Where: - $y(t)$ — process output at discrete time $t$ - $u(t)$ — control input - $e(t)$ — **white noise** disturbance: $E\{e(t)\} = 0$, $E\{e(t)^2\} = \sigma_e^2$ - $k \ge 1$ — **pure time delay** (integer number of sampling intervals) - $z^{-1}$ — backward shift operator The polynomials in $z^{-1}$ are: $$A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}$$ $$B(z^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}, \quad b_0 \ne 0$$ $$C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c} z^{-n_c}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assumption (Standard in KTU module 3):** Polynomials $A(z^{-1})$ and $C(z^{-1})$ are *monic* (leading coefficient 1), $B(z^{-1})$ has no roots on or outside the unit circle (stability & properness), and $e(t)$ is uncorrelated zero-mean white noise. --- ### 2.2 Derivation Strategy of the MV Controller The MV controller emerges from a three-step logical chain: 1. **Derive the optimal $k$-step-ahead predictor** $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t)$ that minimizes $E\{[y(t+k) - \hat{y}(t+k \mid t)]^2\}$. 2. **Set the predictor equal to the desired reference** $w(t+k) = 0$ (regulation problem). 3. **Solve for $u(t)$** — the resulting expression is the MV control law. This is the standard **separation principle** in stochastic control applied to single-step regulators. --- ### 2.3 The Diophantine Equation (Core of MV Design) The pivot of MV design is the **Diophantine identity** (also called the *polynomial division identity*): $$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k} G(z^{-1})$$ Where: - $F(z^{-1}) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + \dots + f_{k-1} z^{-(k-1)}$ — degree $k-1$ (carries the $k$ future noise terms) - $G(z^{-1}) = g_0 + g_1 z^{-1} + \dots + g_{n_g} z^{-n_g}$ — degree $n_a - 1$ - $n_g = \max(n_a - 1,\ n_c - k)$ — degree constraint > [!NOTE] > **Why this identity?** It allows us to "peel off" the $k$ future disturbance terms (which are unknown at time $t$) from the plant equation, isolating the *predictable* part from the *unpredictable* random innovation. --- ### 2.4 The Optimal $k$-Step-Ahead Predictor Using the Diophantine identity, the $k$-step-ahead predictor is: $$\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})} y(t) + \frac{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})} u(t)$$ And the prediction error is **white noise** itself: $$y(t+k) - \hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = F(z^{-1}) e(t+k)$$ The variance of this innovation is: $$E\{[y(t+k) - \hat{y}(t+k \mid t)]^2\} = \sigma_e^2 \left( 1 + f_1^2 + f_2^2 + \dots + f_{k-1}^2 \right)$$ This is the **theoretical minimum** achievable variance — the celebrated **Åström lower bound**. --- ### 2.5 The Minimum Variance Control Law Setting $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = 0$ (regulation to zero) yields: $$G(z^{-1}) y(t) + B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) u(t) = 0$$ Solving for $u(t)$: $$\boxed{\,u(t) = - \frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})}\, y(t)\,}$$ This is the **closed-loop MV controller**. Equivalently in $R$-polynomial form used in self-tuning regulators: $$R(z^{-1}) u(t) = - S(z^{-1}) y(t)$$ where $R = BF$ and $S = G$. --- ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 80% of ESE module-3 marks.** | # | Equation / Identity | Purpose / Role | |---|---|---| | 1 | $A y(t) = z^{-k} B u(t) + C e(t)$ | ARMAX plant model | | 2 | $C = AF + z^{-k} G$ | **Diophantine identity** (core) | | 3 | $\deg F = k-1,\ \deg G = n_a - 1$ | Degree constraints | | 4 | $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = \dfrac{G}{C} y(t) + \dfrac{BF}{C} u(t)$ | $k$-step predictor | | 5 | $y(t+k) - \hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = F e(t+k)$ | Prediction error (white) | | 6 | $u(t) = -\dfrac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})} y(t)$ | **MV control law** | | 7 | $R u(t) = -S y(t)$ with $R = BF$, $S = G$ | $R$-$S$ controller form | | 8 | $E\{F e(t+k)^2\} = \sigma_e^2 (1 + \sum f_i^2)$ | Minimum output variance | | 9 | $C = E A + z^{-k} B D$ (alt. form) | Identity used in MA design | | 10 | $J = E\{y^2(t+k) + \lambda u^2(t) + \dots + \}$ | Generalized cost (MA) | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Trap:** In some textbooks (e.g., Åström & Wittenmark), $F$ and $G$ are defined for $A = EF$ form. In KTU 2024 module-3 notation, the form $C = AF + z^{-k}G$ is used — verify the syllabus style before computing. --- ### 2.7 Extension to the Moving Average (MA) Controller When the minimum-variance control is too aggressive or the plant is non-minimum-phase, the controller is generalized as follows: **Generalized cost function:** $$J = E \left\{ \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left[ y(t+j) - w(t+j) \right]^2 \right\}$$ where $N$ is the **prediction/cost horizon**, and $w$ is the reference. A *moving-average* control law maintains $u$ **constant** over the control horizon $N_u \le N$: $$\Delta u(t) = \Delta u(t+1) = \dots = \Delta u(t+N_u - 1)$$ The corresponding **Diophantine identity for MA design** uses an *incremental* model: $$\Delta A(z^{-1}) y(t) = z^{-k} B(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t) + C(z^{-1}) \Delta e(t)$$ followed by the standard identity $C = \tilde{A} F + z^{-k} G$ with $\tilde{A} = \Delta A$, leading to the same MV-type law, but the cost now spans $N$ future steps. --- ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Paper machines and rolling mills** — stochastic load disturbances justify MV control. - **Chemical reactors** with stochastic feedstock variability. - **Ship steering autopilot** — the historical birthplace of adaptive control (Åström's work at KTH). - **Servo systems with white-noise sensor errors** — MV gives a baseline benchmark against which to evaluate advanced controllers. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From ARMAX to MV Control Law We start from the standard ARMAX plant: $$A(z^{-1}) y(t) = z^{-k} B(z^{-1}) u(t) + C(z^{-1}) e(t) \tag{1}$$ #### Step 1: Rewrite the Plant Equation We wish to predict $y(t+k)$. Multiply both sides by $z^{k}$ (shift forward by $k$): $$A(z^{-1}) y(t+k) = B(z^{-1}) u(t) + C(z^{-1}) e(t+k) \tag{2}$$ #### Step 2: Apply the Diophantine Identity Recall the identity: $$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k} G(z^{-1}) \tag{3}$$ Substitute (3) into the noise term of (2): $$A y(t+k) = B u(t) + \left[ A F + z^{-k} G \right] e(t+k)$$ $$A y(t+k) = B u(t) + A F e(t+k) + z^{-k} G e(t+k) \tag{4}$$ #### Step 3: Isolate the Predictable Part Move the term $AF e(t+k)$ to the left side. Note that $A F e(t+k)$ contains noise terms from instants $t+1$ through $t+k$, which are **not yet realized** at time $t$. They are *unpredictable* at instant $t$ (since $e(t+1), \dots, e(t+k)$ are future). Subtracting $A F e(t+k)$ from both sides of (4): $$A \left[ y(t+k) - F e(t+k) \right] = B u(t) + z^{-k} G e(t+k) \tag{5}$$ #### Step 4: Substitute $z^{-k} G e(t+k) = G e(t)$ Since $z^{-k} e(t+k) = e(t)$: $$A \left[ y(t+k) - F e(t+k) \right] = B u(t) + G e(t) \tag{6}$$ #### Step 5: Express $e(t)$ in Terms of $y(t)$ and $u(t)$ From the original plant (1) shifted by $k$ (divide through by $C$): $$e(t) = \frac{A}{C} y(t) - \frac{z^{-k} B}{C} u(t) \tag{7}$$ Actually a cleaner route: from (1), $$C e(t) = A y(t) - z^{-k} B u(t) \tag{8}$$ Multiply (8) by $G/C$: $$G e(t) = \frac{G}{C} \left[ A y(t) - z^{-k} B u(t) \right] \tag{9}$$ #### Step 6: Combine Equations Substitute (9) into (6): $$A \left[ y(t+k) - F e(t+k) \right] = B u(t) + \frac{G}{C} \left[ A y(t) - z^{-k} B u(t) \right] \tag{10}$$ Expand the right side: $$A \left[ y(t+k) - F e(t+k) \right] = B u(t) + \frac{GA}{C} y(t) - \frac{z^{-k} GB}{C} u(t) \tag{11}$$ #### Step 7: Divide by $A$ (assuming $A$ has no roots on unit circle) $$y(t+k) - F e(t+k) = \frac{B}{A} u(t) + \frac{G}{C} y(t) - \frac{z^{-k} GB}{AC} u(t) \tag{12}$$ Group $u(t)$ terms: $$y(t+k) - F e(t+k) = \frac{G}{C} y(t) + \left[ \frac{B}{A} - \frac{z^{-k} GB}{AC} \right] u(t) \tag{13}$$ A more elegant route: since $B/A - z^{-k}GB/(AC) = BF/C$ (using identity 3 rearranged), we obtain: $$y(t+k) - F e(t+k) = \frac{G}{C} y(t) + \frac{BF}{C} u(t) \tag{14}$$ #### Step 8: Identify the Predictor Define the optimal predictor: $$\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{G}{C} y(t) + \frac{BF}{C} u(t) \tag{15}$$ Then: $$y(t+k) = \hat{y}(t+k \mid t) + F e(t+k) \tag{16}$$ Since $F e(t+k)$ depends only on future noise (uncorrelated with past), $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t)$ is the **MMSE** (minimum mean square error) predictor. #### Step 9: Minimize Output Variance $$E\{y^2(t+k)\} = E\{\hat{y}^2(t+k \mid t)\} + E\{F^2 e^2(t+k)\}$$ The second term is fixed (white noise) — minimum is achieved when the **first term = 0**: $$\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = 0 \tag{17}$$ #### Step 10: Solve for $u(t)$ Setting (15) to zero: $$\frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})} y(t) + \frac{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})} u(t) = 0$$ Multiplying by $C(z^{-1})$: $$G(z^{-1}) y(t) + B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) u(t) = 0$$ Solving for $u(t)$: $$\boxed{\,u(t) = - \frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})}\, y(t)\,} \tag{18}$$ **Variance of the resulting output:** $$E\{y^2(t+k)\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 \left( 1 + f_1^2 + f_2^2 + \dots + f_{k-1}^2 \right) \tag{19}$$ This is the **theoretical minimum** — no other causal controller can do better. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Full MV Design (KTU-style) > **Given:** A plant described by > $$y(t) - 0.9 y(t-1) = 0.5 u(t-2) + e(t) - 0.4 e(t-1)$$ > **Find:** (a) The MV controller, (b) the closed-loop output variance. **Step 1: Identify Polynomials** $$A = 1 - 0.9 z^{-1},\quad B = 0.5,\quad C = 1 - 0.4 z^{-1},\quad k = 2$$ **Step 2: Diophantine Identity** Need $C = A F + z^{-2} G$ with $\deg F = k-1 = 1$, $\deg G = n_a - 1 = 0$. $$F = 1 + f_1 z^{-1}, \quad G = g_0$$ Compute $AF$: $$AF = (1 - 0.9 z^{-1})(1 + f_1 z^{-1}) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} - 0.9 z^{-1} - 0.9 f_1 z^{-2}$$ $$AF = 1 + (f_1 - 0.9) z^{-1} - 0.9 f_1 z^{-2}$$ Then $C - AF = (1 - 0.4 z^{-1}) - AF$ must equal $z^{-2} G = g_0 z^{-2}$: $$C - AF = \left[ -0.4 - (f_1 - 0.9) \right] z^{-1} + \left[ -(-0.9 f_1) - 0 \right] z^{-2}$$ Wait — let me be more careful. We need: $$C - AF = z^{-2} G = g_0 z^{-2}$$ So coefficients of $z^{0}$ and $z^{-1}$ must vanish: - Coefficient of $z^{0}$: $1 - 1 = 0$ ✓ - Coefficient of $z^{-1}$: $-0.4 - (f_1 - 0.9) = 0 \Rightarrow f_1 = 0.5$ - Coefficient of $z^{-2}$: $0 - (-0.9 f_1) = g_0 \Rightarrow g_0 = 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45$ **Step 3: MV Control Law** $$u(t) = - \frac{G}{B F} y(t) = - \frac{0.45}{0.5 (1 + 0.5 z^{-1})} y(t)$$ $$u(t) = - \frac{0.9}{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}} y(t)$$ Equivalently: $$u(t) + 0.5 u(t-1) = - 0.9 y(t) \tag{20}$$ **Step 4: Output Variance** From (19) with $f_1 = 0.5$: $$E\{y^2\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 (1 + 0.5^2) = 1.25 \sigma_e^2$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU exam trick:** If $\sigma_e^2$ is not given in the question, the answer is usually expressed in the form $(1 + f_1^2 + f_2^2 + \dots)\sigma_e^2$. --- ### 3.3 MA Controller — Extension Derivation For the MA controller, we replace the **single-step** cost with: $$J = E\left\{ \sum_{j=k}^{N+k-1} y^2(t+j) \right\} \tag{21}$$ Using the predictor (15) and noting that the future noise contributions $F e(t+j)$ for $j > k$ are *not* independent white noise samples, we obtain a modified Diophantine identity that factorizes $C$ into a *partial* $F$ (of degree $N-1$) and a remainder $G$: $$C(z^{-1}) = \tilde{A}(z^{-1}) F_N(z^{-1}) + z^{-N} G_N(z^{-1}) \tag{22}$$ where $\deg F_N = N-1$, $\deg G_N = n_a - 1$. The control law (for regulation $w=0$): $$u(t) = - \frac{G_N(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F_N(z^{-1})} y(t) \tag{23}$$ **Remark:** As $N \to \infty$, the MA controller approaches a **generalized minimum-variance (GMV)** controller. Setting $N = k$ recovers the **classical MV controller**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Minimum Variance Control — Closed-Loop Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference w] --> SUMM[ Summing Junction] SUMM --> PLANT[ ARMAX Plant
A y = z-k B u + C e] NOISE[ White noise e] --> PLANT PLANT --> Y[ Output y] Y --> FBK[ Feedback line] FBK --> PRED[ k-step Predictor
G by C and BF by C] PRED --> CTRL[ MV Controller
u = -G over BF times y] CTRL --> SUMM Y --> VAR[ Variance Computer
1 + sum fi^2 times sigma-e2] VAR --> PERF[ Min Output Variance] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The output $y$ is fed back to the predictor, which builds the $k$-step-ahead forecast $\hat{y}$. The MV controller generates $u$ to *zero out* this forecast. The closed-loop output then contains only the unavoidable innovation $F e(t+k)$, whose variance is the Åström lower bound. --- ### 4.2 Moving Average Controller — Multi-Horizon Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD REF[ Reference w] --> HOR[ Cost Horizon N] HOR --> COST[ Cost J = sum of y^2 over N steps] Y[ Process Output y] --> DIOPH[ Extended Diophantine
C = A-tilde F-N + z-N G-N] DIOPH --> MA[ MA Controller
u = - G-N over B F-N times y] MA --> HORIZON[ Constant u over N-u steps] HORIZON --> PLANT[ Plant] PLANT --> Y ``` > [!TIP] > **Key feature of MA diagram:** Notice the *constant $u$* block — the MA controller holds the control signal fixed for $N_u$ consecutive samples, producing a *moving-average* action rather than a single aggressive step. --- ### 4.3 Adaptive (Self-Tuning) MV Control — Recursive Identification Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR PLANT2[ ARMAX Plant] --> Y2[ y] Y2 --> ESTIM[ Recursive Least Squares
Identifier estimates A B C] ESTIM --> PARAM[ Estimated Polynomials A-hat B-hat C-hat] PARAM --> DIOPH2[ Online Diophantine Solver] DIOPH2 --> CTRL2[ MV / MA Control Law] CTRL2 --> U2[ u] U2 --> PLANT2 Y2 --> VAR2[ Monitor variance] VAR2 --> SWITCH{ Variance meets spec?} SWITCH -->|Yes| STAY[ Hold controller] SWITCH -->|No| RECOMPUTE[ Recompute controller] RECOMPUTE --> DIOPH2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU relevance:** This is the **Explicit Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** architecture. The identification and control-design stages are separate. In an *implicit* STR, the controller parameters are estimated directly, skipping the Diophantine solve. --- ### 4.4 Comparison Flowchart — MV vs MA Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD START[ Plant + Disturbance] --> Q1{ Is delay k = 1?} Q1 -->|Yes, single step| MV[ Use MV Controller] Q1 -->|No, k greater than 1| Q2{ Plant minimum phase?} Q2 -->|Yes| MV2[ Use MV Controller] Q2 -->|No, non-min phase| MA[ Use MA / GMV Controller] MA --> Q3{ Need smooth control?} Q3 -->|Yes| TUNE[ Tune N and lambda] Q3 -->|No, prioritize output| ACC[ Accept higher variance] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024 Style]** **Q1.** *State and explain the minimum variance control problem for a discrete-time ARMAX plant. What is the Åström lower bound?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** For the ARMAX plant $A(z^{-1})y(t) = z^{-k}B(z^{-1})u(t) + C(z^{-1})e(t)$, the minimum variance regulator chooses $u(t)$ to **minimize the variance of the future output** $E\{y^2(t+k)\}$. The optimal solution is obtained by setting the $k$-step-ahead MMSE predictor $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t)$ to zero, giving the control law $$u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})F(z^{-1})} y(t)$$ where $F$ and $G$ satisfy the Diophantine identity $C = AF + z^{-k}G$. The **Åström lower bound** is the resulting minimum achievable variance: $$E\{y^2(t+k)\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 \left(1 + f_1^2 + f_2^2 + \dots + f_{k-1}^2\right)$$ No causal controller can yield a smaller output variance. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2.** *Differentiate between the minimum variance (MV) controller and the moving average (MA) controller. State one practical limitation of the MV controller that MA overcomes.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | MV Controller | MA Controller | |---|---|---| | Horizon | Single step ($k$) | Multi-step ($N \ge k$) | | Control law | $u = -\frac{G}{BF} y$ | $u = -\frac{G_N}{BF_N} y$ | | Output variance | Theoretical minimum | Slightly higher | | Control effort | Aggressive, large moves | Smoother, bounded | **Practical Limitation:** The MV controller can **destabilize non-minimum-phase plants** and produces **excessively large control moves** in practice. The MA controller overcomes this by extending the prediction/cost horizon and/or constraining the control moves, yielding smoother and more robust action. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model QP Style, Module 3 Internal Choice]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** *For the ARMAX plant* $$y(t) - 0.8 y(t-1) + 0.15 y(t-2) = 0.4 u(t-1) + 0.3 u(t-2) + e(t) - 0.5 e(t-1)$$ *design the minimum variance controller. State the Diophantine identity, find the polynomials $F$ and $G$, and write the explicit control law.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For the same plant, compute the minimum output variance in terms of $\sigma_e^2$. Comment on the practical difficulty if the plant were non-minimum-phase.* **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Step 1 — Identify polynomials:** $$A = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2},\quad B = 0.4 + 0.3 z^{-1},\quad C = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1},\quad k = 1$$ **Step 2 — Diophantine identity with $k=1$:** When $k=1$, $F$ must have degree $k-1 = 0$, so $F = 1$. The identity becomes: $$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) \cdot 1 + z^{-1} G(z^{-1})$$ Thus $G(z^{-1}) = z \left[ C(z^{-1}) - A(z^{-1}) \right]$: $$C - A = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) - (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2}$$ Multiplying by $z$: $$G(z^{-1}) = 0.3 - 0.15 z^{-1}$$ Check degree: $\deg G = 1 = n_a - 1$ ✓ **Step 3 — Control law:** $$u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})} y(t) = -\frac{0.3 - 0.15 z^{-1}}{0.4 + 0.3 z^{-1}} y(t)$$ Cross-multiplying: $$(0.4 + 0.3 z^{-1}) u(t) = -(0.3 - 0.15 z^{-1}) y(t)$$ $$\boxed{\,0.4 u(t) + 0.3 u(t-1) = -0.3 y(t) + 0.15 y(t-1)\,}$$ **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Minimum output variance:** Since $F = 1$, the formula $E\{y^2\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2(1 + \sum f_i^2)$ gives: $$\boxed{\,E\{y^2(t+1)\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2\,}$$ (This is because for $k=1$, there are no future noise terms beyond the current one, so the entire output is *pure white noise* with variance $\sigma_e^2$.) **Step 2 — Comment on non-minimum-phase plants:** For a **non-minimum-phase (NMP)** plant, the polynomial $B(z^{-1})$ has at least one root *outside* the unit circle. In the MV control law $$u(t) = -\frac{G}{B F} y(t)$$ the controller **inherits** these unstable poles, producing: - Bounded control signal may not exist for a *bounded* output reference; - **Internal instability** in the closed loop: although output regulation succeeds, the control signal $u(t)$ can grow unbounded (the so-called "hidden instability" or "internal instability" of MV control); - The poles of the controller include unstable plant zeros — leading to **cancellation** issues in adaptive estimation. Hence, MV control is **theoretically optimal** but **practically fragile** for NMP plants; the MA controller (or GMV) is preferred because it penalizes large control moves and prevents hidden instabilities. **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Hidden Marks:** When asked about non-minimum-phase behavior, do NOT merely say "MV fails". State the precise reason — **internal instability of the control signal due to unstable pole-zero cancellation in $G/(BF)$**. This is the keyword that fetches full marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Style]** **(a)** *Explain with a block diagram the architecture of an explicit self-tuning minimum-variance regulator. Describe each block's role and write the equations executed at each step.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Consider the ARMAX plant* $$y(t) = 0.5 y(t-1) + 0.7 u(t-2) + e(t) + 0.3 e(t-1)$$ *Design the MA controller with prediction horizon $N = 3$. Show the Diophantine identity and derive the control law explicitly.* **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Q.B(a):** The **Explicit Self-Tuning Minimum-Variance Regulator** has four functional blocks: | Block | Role | Equation | |---|---|---| | **Plant** | ARMAX process | $A y(t) = z^{-k} B u(t) + C e(t)$ | | **Recursive Estimator** | Updates $\hat{A}, \hat{B}, \hat{C}$ online | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \epsilon(t)$ with RLS gains | | **Diophantine Solver** | Computes $F$ and $G$ from $\hat{C} = \hat{A} F + z^{-k} G$ | Polynomial long division each step | | **Controller** | Computes $u(t)$ | $u(t) = -\frac{G}{\hat{B} F} y(t)$ | **Equations executed at each sampling instant $t$:** 1. **Sample new data** $y(t)$. 2. **RLS update:** Compute prediction error $$\epsilon(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t-1) \phi(t-1)$$ where $\phi(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_a), u(t-k), \dots, u(t-k-n_b+1), e(t-1), \dots]$. 3. **Update parameter vector:** $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + P(t) \phi(t) \epsilon(t) / [\lambda + \phi^T P \phi]$. 4. **Solve Diophantine** with the *latest* $\hat{C}$ and $\hat{A}$ → obtain $\hat{F}, \hat{G}$. 5. **Compute control:** $$u(t) = -\frac{\hat{G}(z^{-1})}{\hat{B}(z^{-1}) \hat{F}(z^{-1})} y(t)$$ 6. **Wait for next sample**, repeat. **Block Diagram (textual):** ``` [Plant] ──► y(t) ──► [RLS Estimator] ──► [Diophantine Solver] ──► [Controller] ──► u(t) ▲ │ └────────────────────── feedback y(t) ────────────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!NOTE] > **Implicit vs Explicit:** In the *implicit* STR, the controller parameters $R$ and $S$ are *directly* estimated (no Diophantine solve at each step), making it computationally cheaper. The KTU 2024 syllabus includes both; for this question, the explicit form is asked. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Identify the model:** $$A = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1},\quad B = 0.7,\quad C = 1 + 0.3 z^{-1},\quad k = 2$$ **Step 2 — MA Diophantine identity with $N = 3$:** For MA control with horizon $N$, the identity is: $$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1}) F_N(z^{-1}) + z^{-N} G_N(z^{-1})$$ with $\deg F_N = N - 1 = 2$ and $\deg G_N = n_a - 1 = 0$. Let $F_N = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2}$ and $G_N = g_0$. **Step 3 — Compute $AF_N$:** $$AF_N = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2})$$ $$= 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2} - 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.5 f_1 z^{-2} - 0.5 f_2 z^{-3}$$ $$= 1 + (f_1 - 0.5) z^{-1} + (f_2 - 0.5 f_1) z^{-2} - 0.5 f_2 z^{-3}$$ **Step 4 — Equate $C = AF_N + z^{-3} G_N$:** $$1 + 0.3 z^{-1} = 1 + (f_1 - 0.5) z^{-1} + (f_2 - 0.5 f_1) z^{-2} - 0.5 f_2 z^{-3} + g_0 z^{-3}$$ Matching coefficients of $z^{0}, z^{-1}, z^{-2}, z^{-3}$: - $z^{0}$: $1 = 1$ ✓ - $z^{-1}$: $0.3 = f_1 - 0.5 \Rightarrow f_1 = 0.8$ - $z^{-2}$: $0 = f_2 - 0.5 f_1 \Rightarrow f_2 = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4$ - $z^{-3}$: $0 = -0.5 f_2 + g_0 \Rightarrow g_0 = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.2$ **Step 5 — MA Control Law:** $$u(t) = -\frac{G_N}{B F_N} y(t) = -\frac{0.2}{0.7 (1 + 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.4 z^{-2})} y(t)$$ $$u(t) = -\frac{0.2}{0.7 + 0.56 z^{-1} + 0.28 z^{-2}} y(t)$$ Cross-multiplying: $$0.7 u(t) + 0.56 u(t-1) + 0.28 u(t-2) = -0.2 y(t)$$ **Step 6 — Resulting Output Variance (for reference):** $$E\{y^2\}_{\text{MA}} = \sigma_e^2 (1 + f_1^2 + f_2^2) = \sigma_e^2 (1 + 0.64 + 0.16) = 1.8 \sigma_e^2$$ This is **higher** than the pure MV case (with $k=2$ we would use $\deg F = 1$, giving $1.64 \sigma_e^2$), but the control signal is smoother — the classic MV-vs-MA trade-off. **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Q.B(b):** A common student error is to use $\deg F = k-1 = 1$ instead of $\deg F = N-1 = 2$. **Always read the question carefully** — if MA / horizon $N$ is specified, the Diophantine identity uses $N$, **not** $k$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways students lose marks on this topic (compiled from KTU 2019–2024 valuation reports):** > 1. **Confusing $k$ with $N$** — using the wrong prediction horizon in the Diophantine identity. > 2. **Forgetting the $z^{-k}$ term** in the identity $C = AF + z^{-k}G$ — students write $C = AF + G$. > 3. **Wrong degree constraint** — writing $\deg G = n_a$ instead of $n_a - 1$. > 4. **Inverting $C$ carelessly** — the predictor has $G/C$, but in the *control law* $C$ cancels and only $G$ and $B$ remain. > 5. **Ignoring internal instability** when discussing NMP plants — examiners specifically test this awareness. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Read this checklist 30 minutes before the exam — covers 90% of the module-3 MV/MA marks.** - [ ] **Plant form** — ARMAX: $A y = z^{-k} B u + C e$; identify $A$, $B$, $C$, $k$ correctly. - [ ] **Diophantine identity** — $C = AF + z^{-k}G$, with $\deg F = k-1$, $\deg G = n_a - 1$. - [ ] **$k$-step predictor** — $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = (G/C) y(t) + (BF/C) u(t)$. - [ ] **MV control law** — $u(t) = -\frac{G}{B F} y(t)$; equivalently $R u = -S y$ with $R=BF$, $S=G$. - [ ] **Minimum variance** — $\sigma_e^2(1 + f_1^2 + \dots + f_{k-1}^2)$ — Åström's lower bound. - [ ] **MA controller** — replaces $k$ with horizon $N$ in the Diophantine identity; smoother but higher output variance. - [ ] **R-S polynomial form** — useful for self-tuning regulator implementation. - [ ] **Adaptive / self-tuning** — explicit STR: estimate → Diophantine → control; implicit STR: estimate $R$ and $S$ directly. - [ ] **Non-minimum-phase caveat** — MV can produce hidden instability; MA / GMV preferred. - [ ] **Diophantine long-division technique** — match coefficients of $z^{-i}$ step by step; this is the key computation in the exam. ---

topics.title: Stochastic Self Tuning Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** is an adaptive controller that combines on-line parameter estimation of a stochastic (noise-corrupted) dynamical plant with a recursively re-tuned control law, such that the closed-loop system asymptotically approaches the optimal stochastic regulator performance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the STR is built on three pillars:

1. A **stochastic plant model** — typically the **ARMAX (Auto-Regressive Moving Average with eXogenous input)** structure.
2. An **on-line parameter estimator** — most commonly the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** algorithm (or its variants like RLS with forgetting factor / Kalman filter / stochastic approximation).
3. A **control design block** — most commonly the **Minimum Variance Controller (MVC)** or **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)** controller, whose parameters are updated at every sampling instant using the latest plant parameter estimates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The stochastic STR is the cornerstone of KTU Module 3 because it relaxes the deterministic assumption of Module 2 (Model Reference Adaptive Control) and explicitly accounts for **colored measurement noise**, **stochastic disturbances**, and **uncertain plant parameters**.

Mathematically, a generic stochastic SISO plant is represented as:

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,u(t-k) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

where $\omega(t)$ is a **zero-mean white noise** sequence with variance $\sigma^{2}$, $k$ is the inherent **time delay**, and $u(t), y(t)$ are the input and output respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Noisy Cruise Control:**
> Imagine driving on a Kerala highway with cruise control engaged. The road has bumps, the wind gusts, and the speedometer has a small jittery noise overlay. A **deterministic** adaptive controller would pretend the speed reading is exact and aggressively re-tune the throttle. A **stochastic** self-tuning regulator, however, *knows* the speed signal is noisy. It:
> (a) first **filters / estimates** the true speed by averaging past noisy readings (parameter estimation),
> (b) then **recomputes** the throttle setting (control law) assuming its estimates are the true values (certainty equivalence),
> (c) and does this every fraction of a second.
>
> The result: smoother, fuel-efficient driving that converges to the target speed despite the noise — exactly what a stochastic STR does for industrial processes.

### 1.3 Why Stochastic STR is Important

- Handles **realistic industrial environments** where noise is unavoidable (sensor quantization, actuator drift, load disturbances).
- The **Minimum Variance** objective is mathematically tractable in ARMAX form, leading to a clean, implementable control law.
- Foundation for **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** adaptive variants, **self-tuning predictors**, and **adaptive Kalman filtering**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key metric for KTU:** The performance benchmark for any stochastic STR is the theoretical **minimum output variance** $J_{\min} = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$, which is achieved only when plant parameters are known exactly. The STR attempts to reach this bound asymptotically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of a Stochastic STR

A stochastic STR can be decomposed into three interacting subsystems operating at every sampling instant $t$:

- **Block A — Plant (ARMAX form):**
  $$
  A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
  $$

- **Block B — Recursive Parameter Estimator (e.g., RLS):**
  Uses past input–output data to produce an estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ of the unknown parameter vector $\theta$.

- **Block C — Control Law Synthesizer (e.g., Minimum Variance or GMV):**
  Uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to compute the control signal $u(t)$ that minimizes the chosen cost function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Certainty Equivalence Principle:** When the estimator is replaced by its converged estimate, the STR behaves as if the true parameters are known. This principle is what allows the design to be *separable* into an estimation problem and a control problem.

### 2.2 The ARMAX Plant Model (Detailed)

For an SISO system with delay $k \geq 1$:

$$
A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_{1}z^{-1} + a_{2}z^{-2} + \cdots + a_{n_{a}}z^{-n_{a}}
$$

$$
B(z^{-1}) = b_{0} + b_{1}z^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_{b}}z^{-n_{b}}
$$

$$
C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_{1}z^{-1} + c_{2}z^{-2} + \cdots + c_{n_{c}}z^{-n_{c}}
$$

- The **$A$ polynomial** captures the output's auto-regressive dynamics.
- The **$B$ polynomial** captures the actuator-to-output path (including any zero-order hold effect).
- The **$C$ polynomial** models the **colored noise** that drives the system. When $C(z^{-1}) = 1$, the noise is white and the model collapses to ARX.

### 2.3 The Minimum Variance Control (MVC) Law

For the ARMAX plant, the **$k$-step ahead optimal predictor** of $y(t+k)$ is:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + G(z^{-1})\,y(t)
$$

where $F(z^{-1})$ and $G(z^{-1})$ are the unique polynomials obtained from the **Diophantine identity**:

$$
C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,G(z^{-1})
$$

with $F$ of degree $k-1$ and $G$ of degree $n_{a}-1$.

The **Minimum Variance Control law** is obtained by setting the predicted output to zero:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $k$-step ahead?** In the stochastic case, only the $k$-step ahead prediction is independent of future noise $\omega(t), \omega(t+1), \ldots, \omega(t+k-1)$. Thus the prediction error variance $E\{[y(t+k) - \hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t)]^{2}\}$ becomes the unavoidable lower bound on output variance.

### 2.4 Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Estimator

The unknown parameter vector is:

$$
\theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_{1} & a_{2} & \cdots & a_{n_{a}} & b_{0} & b_{1} & \cdots & b_{n_{b}} & c_{1} & c_{2} & \cdots & c_{n_{c}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

with corresponding regression vector $\varphi(t-1)$. The RLS update law is:

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\hat{\theta}(t-1)\bigr]
$$

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}
$$

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\Bigl[I - K(t)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\,P(t-1)
$$

where $0 < \lambda \leq 1$ is the **forgetting factor** (commonly $0.95 \leq \lambda \leq 1$).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | KTU-Standard Form |
|---|---|---|
| Plant | Stochastic SISO ARMAX | $A y(t) = B z^{-k} u(t) + C \omega(t)$ |
| Noise | Zero-mean white | $\omega(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$, **$\sigma^{2}$ = noise variance** |
| Diophantine identity | Defines MVC polynomials | $C = A F + z^{-k} G$ with $\deg F = k-1$ |
| Minimum variance cost | Performance index | $J = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$ |
| Minimum cost value | Achievable bound | $J_{\min} = \sigma^{2}\bigl(1 + f_{1}^{2} + f_{2}^{2} + \cdots + f_{k-1}^{2}\bigr)$ |
| Control law | MVC for known plant | $u(t) = -G\,y(t) / (B\,F)$ |
| STR control law | MVC with estimates | $u(t) = -\hat{G}\,y(t) / (\hat{B}\,\hat{F})$ |
| RLS gain | Update weight | $K(t) = P(t-1)\varphi / (\lambda + \varphi^{T} P \varphi)$ |
| Covariance update | Forgetting factor form | $P(t) = [I - K \varphi^{T}] P(t-1) / \lambda$ |
| Stability condition | Persistent excitation | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t)\,\varphi^{T}(t) \succ 0$ |
| Convergence condition | Projected RLS | $\Vert \hat{\theta}(t) - \theta \Vert \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Stochastic STRs are deployed in **paper mills** (basis weight control), **petrochemical reactors** (composition control with noisy analyzers), and **autonomous vehicles** (lane-keeping under sensor noise). The MVC cost $J_{\min}$ is the irreducible variance — the controller cannot reduce output variance below this, even with perfect knowledge of parameters.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Adaptive control of distillation columns, heat exchangers, and CSTRs where stochastic disturbances dominate.
- **Aerospace:** Adaptive autopilots operating in turbulent air (modeled as stochastic disturbances).
- **Robotics:** Joint-position control with noisy encoder feedback.
- **Econometrics / Finance:** Adaptive forecasting of stochastic time series using the same ARMAX/RLS toolbox.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Variance Predictor

**Step 1 — Start from the ARMAX plant model.**

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

Rearranging for $y(t+k)$:

$$
y(t+k) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose $C/A$ into a $k$-step-ahead predictable part and a moving-average remainder.**

Since the noise is white, $\omega(t+1), \omega(t+2), \ldots, \omega(t+k)$ are *not* known at time $t$, but $\omega(t), \omega(t-1), \ldots$ are. We therefore divide $C(z^{-1})$ by $A(z^{-1})$ to obtain:

$$
\frac{C(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})} = F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}
$$

where $\deg F = k-1$ and $\deg G = n_{a}-1$. Multiplying through by $A(z^{-1})$ gives the **Diophantine identity**:

$$
C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,G(z^{-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the output expression.**

$$
y(t+k) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

The last term has $z^{-k}$, meaning it depends on $\omega(t), \omega(t-1), \ldots$ — values that *are* known at time $t$. The middle term $F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k)$ depends on $\omega(t+k), \omega(t+k-1), \ldots, \omega(t+1)$, which are *unknown*.

**Step 4 — Identify the optimal predictor.**

The **$k$-step-ahead minimum-variance predictor** is the part that depends only on known quantities:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

But $\omega$ itself is unknown; using $A\,\omega = A\,y - B\,z^{-k}\,u$ (from the plant equation) we rewrite:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

This is the standard form shown in Section 2.

**Step 5 — Compute the prediction error and its variance.**

The prediction error is:

$$
\tilde{y}(t+k \mid t) = y(t+k) - \hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k)
$$

Since $\omega$ is white with variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$
E\{\tilde{y}^{2}(t+k \mid t)\} = \sigma^{2}\bigl(1 + f_{1}^{2} + f_{2}^{2} + \cdots + f_{k-1}^{2}\bigr) = J_{\min}
$$

This is the **irreducible minimum variance** of the closed-loop output — the lower bound that no controller can beat.

**Step 6 — Derive the minimum variance control law.**

The control objective is $J = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$. The optimal choice at each instant is to set $\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = 0$, which gives:

$$
\frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})\,}\,y(t) = 0
$$

Solving for $u(t)$ yields the **MVC law**:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The polynomials $F$ and $G$ are obtained by long division of $C$ by $A$, carried out exactly to $k-1$ quotient terms plus a $z^{-k}$ remainder. This is the **Diophantine equation** solution — a high-yield KTU exam item.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Update

**Step 1 — Stack the ARMAX equation into a linear regression form.**

Define the regression vector at instant $t$:

$$
\varphi^{T}(t) = \bigl[-y(t), -y(t-1), \ldots, -y(t-n_{a}+1),\,u(t-k),\ldots,u(t-k-n_{b}+1),\,\omega(t),\ldots\bigr]
$$

and the parameter vector:

$$
\theta^{T} = \bigl[a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n_{a}},\,b_{0}, b_{1}, \ldots, b_{n_{b}},\,c_{1}, c_{2}, \ldots, c_{n_{c}}\bigr]
$$

Then the plant model is compactly:

$$
y(t) = \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta + \omega(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the off-line least-squares cost.**

$$
V_{N}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr]^{2}
$$

Setting $\partial V_{N}/\partial \theta = 0$ gives:

$$
\Bigl[\sum_{t=1}^{N}\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\,\theta = \sum_{t=1}^{N}\varphi(t-1)\,y(t)
$$

The off-line solution is $\theta_{N} = P_{N}^{-1}\,b_{N}$ where $P_{N} = \sum \varphi\varphi^{T}$ and $b_{N} = \sum \varphi y$.

**Step 3 — Introduce the forgetting factor.**

For time-varying plants, weight recent data more heavily:

$$
V_{N}^{\lambda}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr]^{2}, \qquad 0 < \lambda \leq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to recursive form.**

Define:

$$
P^{-1}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\lambda^{t-i}\,\varphi(i-1)\,\varphi^{T}(i-1)
$$

Then:

$$
P^{-1}(t) = \lambda\,P^{-1}(t-1) + \varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)
$$

Applying the **matrix inversion lemma** $(A + uv^{T})^{-1} = A^{-1} - \dfrac{A^{-1}uv^{T}A^{-1}}{1 + v^{T}A^{-1}u}$ with $A = \lambda P^{-1}(t-1)$ and $u = v = \varphi(t-1)$ yields:

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\Bigl[I - \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}\Bigr]\,P(t-1)
$$

Defining the **gain vector**:

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}
$$

the parameter update becomes:

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\hat{\theta}(t-1)\bigr]
$$

The bracketed term is the **prediction error** $e(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid t-1)$, which is measurable online.

### 3.3 The Combined STR Algorithm (Implementation)

At each sampling instant $t$, the STR executes the following steps in order:

**Step 1 — Measure** $y(t)$.
**Step 2 — Form** the regression vector $\varphi(t-1)$.
**Step 3 — Predict** using current estimates: $\hat{y}(t) = \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\hat{\theta}(t-1)$.
**Step 4 — Compute** the prediction error: $e(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t)$.
**Step 5 — Update** parameters using the RLS formulas.
**Step 6 — Solve** the Diophantine identity $\hat{C} = \hat{A}\hat{F} + z^{-k}\hat{G}$ with the *new* estimates.
**Step 7 — Compute** the control signal:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{\hat{G}(z^{-1})}{\hat{B}(z^{-1})\,\hat{F}(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

**Step 8 — Apply** $u(t)$ to the plant, increment $t \to t+1$, and loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Diophantine identity in Step 6 must be re-solved *at every* sampling instant with the freshly updated $\hat{A}, \hat{C}$. Students often forget this and use a fixed pre-computed $F, G$, which destroys adaptivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r t] --> SUMC[Sum Node Controller]
    Y[Plant Output y t] --> SUMC
    SUMC --> E[Error e t]
    E --> CTRL[Control Law MVC Block]
    CTRL --> UT[Control Signal u t]
    UT --> PLANT[(ARMAX Plant)]
    PLANT --> YT[Output y t]
    PLANT --> WT[White Noise w t]

    Y --> RLS[RLS Parameter Estimator]
    UT --> RLS
    RLS --> THETA[Estimated Parameters theta hat]
    THETA --> CTRL

    THETA --> DIO[Diophantine Solver F G]
    DIO --> CTRL

    YT --> YB[Output Buffer]
    UT --> UB[Input Buffer]
    YB --> RLS
    UB --> RLS
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Inside the RLS Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[New Measurement y t] --> ERR[Compute Prediction Error e t]
    PHI[Regression Vector phi t-1] --> ERR
    PCOV[Old Covariance P t-1] --> GAIN[Compute Kalman Gain K t]
    PHI --> GAIN
    PCOV --> GAIN
    GAIN --> UPD[Update theta hat t]
    THETAOLD[Old Estimate theta hat t-1] --> UPD
    ERR --> UPD
    UPD --> THETANEW[New Estimate theta hat t]
    UPD --> PNEW[New Covariance P t]
    PNEW --> PCOV
    THETANEW --> THETAOLD
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Interaction Between Plant, Estimator, and Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANT_BLOCK[ARMAX Plant]
      AOUT[Output Channel A z-1]
      BOUT[Input Channel B z-1 z-k]
      NOUT[Noise Channel C z-1]
    end

    subgraph ESTIMATOR_BLOCK[Recursive Estimator]
      REGR[Regressor phi]
      RLS_ALGO[RLS Algorithm]
      PEST[Parameter Estimate]
    end

    subgraph CONTROLLER_BLOCK[Minimum Variance Controller]
      DIO[Diophantine Solver]
      MVC[Control Law u t]
    end

    BOUT --> AOUT
    NOUT --> AOUT
    AOUT --> REGR
    REGR --> RLS_ALGO
    RLS_ALGO --> PEST
    PEST --> DIO
    DIO --> MVC
    MVC --> BOUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams show three coupled loops operating at the same sampling rate: the **outer control loop** (plant $\rightarrow$ controller $\rightarrow$ plant), the **inner estimation loop** (plant outputs $\rightarrow$ RLS $\rightarrow$ parameter update), and the **adaptive law loop** (estimates $\rightarrow$ Diophantine $\rightarrow$ controller). The convergence of all three loops determines the success of the STR.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator. State the three main blocks of an STR and briefly explain the role of the certainty equivalence principle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) is an adaptive controller that adjusts its control law in real time based on online estimates of the parameters of a stochastic (noise-affected) plant, so as to drive the closed-loop output variance toward its theoretical minimum.

The three main blocks are:

1. **Parameter Estimator** (e.g., RLS) — estimates plant parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ from past input–output data.
2. **Control Design Block** (e.g., Minimum Variance Controller) — uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to design the current control law $u(t)$.
3. **Plant** (ARMAX form) — the actual stochastic process being controlled.

The **certainty equivalence principle** states that the control law computed using the *estimated* parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ is implemented as if $\hat{\theta}(t)$ were the *true* parameter $\theta$. This decouples estimation from control design, making the STR implementable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Write the ARMAX model of a stochastic SISO plant and identify the role of each polynomial $A(z^{-1})$, $B(z^{-1})$ and $C(z^{-1})$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

- **$A(z^{-1})$** — Autoregressive polynomial: encodes the *output-side dynamics* (poles, internal feedback) of the plant.
- **$B(z^{-1})$** — Input polynomial: encodes the *actuator-to-output* path, including zeros introduced by the input.
- **$C(z^{-1})$** — Noise polynomial: shapes the *colored disturbance* acting on the system. When $C(z^{-1}) = 1$, the disturbance reduces to pure white noise and the model collapses to ARX.
- $\omega(t)$ is a **zero-mean white noise** with variance $\sigma^{2}$, and $k$ is the **system delay** in samples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either-Or choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — 7 marks]**
*Consider a stochastic SISO plant with delay $k = 1$ and ARMAX form:*
$$
(1 - 0.8\,z^{-1})\,y(t) = 0.5\,z^{-1}\,u(t) + (1 - 0.4\,z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$
